topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT503 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multivariate Normal Distribution Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Multivariate Normal Distribution Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Multivariate Normal Distribution (MVN)**, also known as the **Multivariate Gaussian Distribution**, is a generalization of the one-dimensional normal distribution to higher dimensions. Formally, a $p$-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p)^T$ follows a multivariate normal distribution if every linear combination of its components is normally distributed.

**Definition via Moment Generating Function (MGF):**
A random vector $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ has a multivariate normal distribution with mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ (positive definite) if its MGF is given by:

$$M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{t}) = E\left[e^{\mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{X}}\right] = \exp\left(\mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}\right)$$

We denote this as $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** The MGF-based definition is the most rigorous and is universally preferred in board examinations because it works even when $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is singular (degenerate case).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a quality control engineer measuring the **diameter** and **weight** of a manufactured ball bearing. Each bearing gives you a *pair* of measurements $(x, y)$ on a scatter plot. Due to small random errors in the machines, these points will cluster around a central point — this cluster forms an elliptical "cloud" in 2D space. The Multivariate Normal Distribution is the mathematical equation that describes the *shape*, *center*, and *spread* of this cloud.

- The **center** of the cloud is the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mu_1, \mu_2)^T$.
- The **shape and orientation** of the ellipse is governed by the covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.
- The **density** of points is highest at the center and tapers off outward in a bell-shaped manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** In 1D, the normal distribution is a "bell curve." In 2D, it is a 3D bell-shaped surface (like a mountain) viewed from above as concentric ellipses. In 3D, it is a 4D-hypersurface whose level sets are ellipsoids.

### 1.3 Density Function (For Non-Singular Case)

When the covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is positive definite (non-singular), the probability density function (PDF) exists and is given by:

$$f_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$$

where $\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert$ denotes the determinant of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$ is its inverse.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of the MVN

The Multivariate Normal Distribution is built upon four foundational pillars that every KTU examiner expects a student to know:

### (A) The Mean Vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$
- A $p \times 1$ column vector containing the expected value of each component.
- $E[\mathbf{X}] = \boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_p)^T$

### (B) The Covariance Matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$
- A $p \times p$ symmetric, positive semi-definite matrix.
- Diagonal entries $\sigma_{ii}$ are the variances of $X_i$.
- Off-diagonal entries $\sigma_{ij}$ are the covariances between $X_i$ and $X_j$.

### (C) The Mahalanobis Distance
- The quadratic form $(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$ is called the **Mahalanobis distance** $D^2$.
- It measures how many "standard deviations" $\mathbf{x}$ is from the mean, accounting for correlation.

### (D) The Normalization Constant
- $(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}$ ensures the PDF integrates to 1 over $\mathbb{R}^p$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Notes / Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| Notation | $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ | $p$-dimensional MVN |
| MGF | $M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{t}) = \exp\left(\mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}\right)$ | Valid for all $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ |
| PDF | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | Requires $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ positive definite |
| Mean Vector | $E[\mathbf{X}] = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | First moment |
| Covariance Matrix | $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ | Second central moment |
| Linear Combination | $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{X} \sim N(\mathbf{a}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{a}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{a})$ | For any constant vector $\mathbf{a}$ |
| Mahalanobis $D^2$ | $D^2 = (\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim \chi^2_p$ | Chi-squared with $p$ d.f. |
| Standardization | $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{L}^{-1}(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{L}^T$ (Cholesky) |
| Partitioned Vector | $\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{X}_1 \\ \mathbf{X}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \\ \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22} \end{pmatrix}$ | Partition dimensions $q$ and $p-q$ |
| Marginal Dist. | $\mathbf{X}_1 \sim N_q(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11})$ | Sub-vector remains MVN |
| Conditional Dist. | $\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 \sim N_q(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1 \vert 2}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{1 \vert 2})$ | See formulas below |
| Cond. Mean | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1 \vert 2} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} (\mathbf{X}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$ | Regression form |
| Cond. Variance | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{1 \vert 2} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}$ | Schur complement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Machine Learning, the MVN is the backbone of **Gaussian Naive Bayes classifiers**, **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)**, and **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)**. In signal processing, it models **noise distributions** in radar and communication systems.

## 2.3 Properties of the Multivariate Normal Distribution

1. **Linear Closure:** Any linear transformation of an MVN vector is itself MVN. If $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b}$, then $\mathbf{Y} \sim N_m(\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T)$.

2. **Marginal Normality:** Every sub-vector of an MVN vector is also MVN.

3. **Conditional Normality:** Conditional distributions of sub-vectors are MVN.

4. **Zero Covariance $\Rightarrow$ Independence:** For MVN, $\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0$ if and only if $X_i$ and $X_j$ are independent. (This is **NOT** true in general!)

5. **Contour Ellipsoids:** The level sets $f(\mathbf{x}) = c$ are ellipsoids centered at $\boldsymbol{\mu}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MGF of the Bivariate Normal Distribution

Let $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2)^T \sim N_2(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ where:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} \mu_1 \\ \mu_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \\ \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 & \sigma_2^2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Write the definition of the MGF.
$$M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{t}) = E\left[\exp(t_1 X_1 + t_2 X_2)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $Y = t_1 X_1 + t_2 X_2$. Since $Y$ is a linear combination of jointly normal random variables, $Y$ itself is normally distributed.

**Step 3:** Compute the mean and variance of $Y$.
$$E[Y] = t_1 \mu_1 + t_2 \mu_2 = \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

$$\text{Var}(Y) = t_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + 2 t_1 t_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + t_2^2 \sigma_2^2 = \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the MGF of a univariate normal $Y \sim N(\mu_Y, \sigma_Y^2)$, which is $M_Y(t) = \exp(\mu_Y t + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_Y^2 t^2)$.

Replacing $t$ with 1 (since $Y$ is already a complete expression):
$$M_{\mathbf{X}}(t_1, t_2) = \exp\left(\mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}\right)$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Conditional Distribution of MVN

Let $\mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{X}_1^T, \mathbf{X}_2^T)^T$ be partitioned as before. We want to find $f(\mathbf{x}_1 \mid \mathbf{x}_2)$.

**Step 1:** Apply the factorization rule.
$$f(\mathbf{x}_1 \mid \mathbf{x}_2) = \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2)}{f(\mathbf{x}_2)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the bivariate MVN density for the joint and univariate MVN for the marginal.

**Step 3:** After algebraic simplification (combining quadratic forms in the exponent), the cross-terms in $\mathbf{x}_1 \mathbf{x}_2$ that remain in the joint but not the marginal produce the conditional mean.

**Step 4:** The result is:
$$\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 \sim N_q\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2), \; \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}\right)$$

The conditional covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{1 \mid 2}$ is the **Schur complement** of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}$ in $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Mahalanobis Distance Follows Chi-Squared

**Step 1:** Standardize $\mathbf{X}$ using the Cholesky decomposition $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{L}^T$.
$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{L}^{-1}(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$$

**Step 2:** The components of $\mathbf{Z}$ are independent standard normals.

**Step 3:** Compute the squared norm:
$$D^2 = (\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \mathbf{Z}^T \mathbf{Z} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} Z_i^2$$

**Step 4:** By definition, the sum of $p$ independent $\chi^2_1$ random variables follows $\chi^2_p$.
$$D^2 \sim \chi^2_p$$

This is a critical result used to construct **confidence ellipsoids** in multivariate statistics. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: MVN Ecosystem and Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Multivariate Normal X ~ N_p mu Sigma"] --> B["Linear Transformation Y = CX + b"]
    A --> C["Partitioning X1 and X2"]
    A --> D["Standardization via Cholesky"]
    A --> E["Mahalanobis Distance D squared"]

    B --> B1["Y ~ N_m C mu plus b, C Sigma C transpose"]
    C --> C1["Marginal: X1 ~ N_q mu1, Sigma11"]
    C --> C2["Conditional: X1 given X2 ~ N"]
    D --> D1["Z = L_inv X minus mu ~ N_p 0, I"]
    E --> E1["D squared ~ Chi squared p d.f."]

    C2 --> C2a["Cond Mean: mu1 plus Sigma12 Sigma22_inv x2 minus mu2"]
    C2 --> C2b["Cond Var: Schur complement Sigma11 minus Sigma12 Sigma22_inv Sigma21"]

    D1 --> F["Independent Standard Normals Z1 to Zp"]
    F --> E1
    E1 --> G["Confidence Ellipsoids in R power p"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: PDF Structure Breakdown

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PDF f of x"] --> N["Normalization Constant"]
    P --> E["Exponential Kernel"]
    N --> N1["1 over 2 pi power p/2"]
    N --> N2["Determinant of Sigma power 1/2"]
    E --> E1["Negative 1/2"]
    E --> E2["Mahalanobis Quadratic Form"]
    E2 --> E2a["x minus mu transpose"]
    E2 --> E2b["Sigma inverse"]
    E2 --> E2c["x minus mu"]

    N1 --> OUT["Fully Specified Density"]
    N2 --> OUT
    E1 --> OUT
    E2a --> OUT
    E2b --> OUT
    E2c --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Geometric Interpretation of Level Sets

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G["2D MVN Level Sets"] --> L1["Center at mu1, mu2"]
    G --> L2["Shape: Ellipses"]
    G --> L3["Axes Aligned with Eigenvectors of Sigma"]
    G --> L4["Axis Lengths proportional to sqrt eigenvalues of Sigma"]
    G --> L5["Highest density at center"]
    G --> L6["Density decays as Mahalanobis D squared increases"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Multivariate Normal Distribution using the moment generating function. State the conditions on the mean vector and covariance matrix.**

**Model Answer:**
A $p$-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p)^T$ is said to follow a multivariate normal distribution with mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, denoted as $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, if its moment generating function is given by:

$$M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{t}) = E[e^{\mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{X}}] = \exp\left(\mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}\right)$$

**Conditions:**
- $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the mean vector with $E[\mathbf{X}] = \boldsymbol{\mu}$.
- $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is a $p \times p$ symmetric, positive semi-definite matrix representing the covariance structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Tip:** Always state the dimensionality ($p$), the notation, and the conditions on $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. Missing the positive semi-definite condition costs a mark.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain any three properties of the Multivariate Normal Distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Linear Transformation Closure:** If $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b}$, then $\mathbf{Y} \sim N_m(\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T)$ for any constant matrix $\mathbf{C}$ and vector $\mathbf{b}$.

2. **Marginal Normality:** Any sub-vector $\mathbf{X}_1$ of $\mathbf{X}$ follows its own MVN: $\mathbf{X}_1 \sim N_q(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11})$.

3. **Zero Covariance Implies Independence:** For MVN vectors, $\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0$ if and only if $X_i$ and $X_j$ are independent. (This is unique to MVN — not true in general.)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Derive the conditional distribution of $\mathbf{X}_1$ given $\mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2$ for a partitioned multivariate normal random vector.**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Setup and Conditional Density

Let $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ be partitioned as:
$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{X}_1 \\ \mathbf{X}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \\ \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** The conditional density is $f(\mathbf{x}_1 \mid \mathbf{x}_2) = \dfrac{f(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2)}{f(\mathbf{x}_2)}$.

**Step 2:** Write the joint density:
$$f(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$$

**Step 3:** Write the marginal density of $\mathbf{x}_2$:
$$f(\mathbf{x}_2) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{(p-q)/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)\right)$$

**Step 4:** Take the ratio and simplify the quadratic forms. The cross-terms involving $\mathbf{x}_1$ and $\mathbf{x}_2$ yield the conditional mean, and the determinant ratio simplifies to $\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{1 \mid 2} \vert$.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Final Conditional Distribution

**Step 5:** The resulting conditional distribution is:

$$\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 \sim N_q\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2), \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Identify the **conditional mean**:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1 \mid 2} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$$

This is the **Best Linear Predictor (BLP)** of $\mathbf{X}_1$ given $\mathbf{X}_2$.

**Step 7:** Identify the **conditional covariance matrix (Schur Complement)**:
$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{1 \mid 2} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{1 \mid 2}$ with $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11}$. Remember: the conditional variance is the *reduction* in uncertainty — it is the *original* variance minus a positive semi-definite correction term. Forgetting to subtract leads to zero marks in part (b).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

**If $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b}$, prove that $\mathbf{Y} \sim N_m(\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T)$ using the MGF method.**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): MGF of Y

**Step 1:** Define the MGF of $\mathbf{Y}$ for any $\mathbf{s} \in \mathbb{R}^m$:
$$M_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{s}) = E[e^{\mathbf{s}^T \mathbf{Y}}]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b}$:
$$M_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{s}) = E\left[e^{\mathbf{s}^T (\mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b})}\right] = e^{\mathbf{s}^T \mathbf{b}} \cdot E\left[e^{(\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})^T \mathbf{X}}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the inner expectation as $M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})$. Apply the MGF formula for $\mathbf{X}$:
$$M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s}) = \exp\left((\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})^T \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} (\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using transpose identities: $(\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})^T \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{s}^T \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $(\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} (\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s}) = \mathbf{s}^T \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T \mathbf{s}$.

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Identifying the Distribution

**Step 5:** Combine:
$$M_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{s}) = \exp\left(\mathbf{s}^T (\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}) + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{s}^T (\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T) \mathbf{s}\right)$$

**Step 6:** By the uniqueness of MGFs, this is the MGF of $N_m(\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T)$. Hence:
$$\mathbf{Y} \sim N_m(\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T) \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common error is to write $\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}$ instead of $\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T$. The covariance of $\mathbf{Y}$ is **always** of the form $\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T$ — this is the "sandwich" form. Losing this transpose costs full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Always quote the MGF-based definition $M_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{t}) = \exp(\mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t})$.
- **Notation:** $\mathbf{X} \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ — remember the subscript $p$ for dimension.
- **PDF Exists** only if $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is positive definite (i.e., $\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert > 0$).
- **Mean vector** is $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ (a $p \times 1$ column), and **covariance matrix** is $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (a $p \times p$ symmetric matrix).
- **Linear transformation** $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b} \sim N_m(\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T)$.
- **Marginal distribution** of any sub-vector $\mathbf{X}_1$ is $\mathbf{X}_1 \sim N_q(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11})$.
- **Conditional mean** is $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$ — this is the **multivariate regression** formula.
- **Conditional variance** is the **Schur complement** $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}$.
- **Mahalanobis distance squared** $D^2 = (\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim \chi^2_p$.
- **Independence ↔ Zero Covariance** is **unique to MVN** — use this fact liberally in proofs.
- **Contour plots** of MVN density are ellipsoids centered at $\boldsymbol{\mu}$.
- **Standardization** uses the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{L}^{-1}(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.
- **Determinant and Inverse** of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ always appear as $\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$ in the PDF.
- **Sandwich form** $\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}^T$ is the only valid form for the transformed covariance — never write $\mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{C}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT503 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Estimation of Parameters?

**Estimation of Parameters** is the statistical procedure of inferring the unknown numerical characteristics (called **parameters**) of a probability distribution from which a sample of observations has been drawn. The true value of the parameter is unobservable; we use sample data to "guess" it as accurately as possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A **parameter** is a numerical characteristic of a *population* (e.g., population mean $\mu$, population variance $\sigma^2$, probability $p$ of a Bernoulli trial). An **estimator** is a *rule* (a function of the sample) used to approximate the parameter. The *value* the estimator takes for a given sample is called an **estimate**.

## 1.2 Two Branches of Estimation

| Branch | Output | KTU Board View |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Estimation** | A single best-value number $\hat{\theta}$ | "Guess the unknown $\theta$ by one number" |
| **Interval Estimation** | A range $[\hat{\theta}_L, \hat{\theta}_U]$ with a stated confidence level | "Give a bracket likely to contain $\theta$" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dark Room" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Fishing in a Dark Lake**
> Imagine the true parameter $\theta$ is a *single golden fish* swimming somewhere inside a large, dark lake (the population). We cannot drain the lake to find it, so we drop a **net (the sample)** at random spots.
> - A **point estimate** is the single fish we pull up — we *declare* that to be the location of the golden fish.
> - An **interval estimate** is when, instead of declaring one spot, we drag a **dragnet across a region** and say, "We are 95% sure the golden fish is inside this swept region."
> - **Standard error** tells us how *wide* our dragnet needs to be to be confident.

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary Used by KTU Examiners

- **Statistic:** A function of the sample alone (no unknown parameters). Example: $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$.
- **Estimator:** A statistic chosen to approximate a parameter; denoted $\hat{\theta}$ (theta-hat).
- **Sampling Distribution:** The probability distribution of an estimator over all possible samples of size $n$.
- **Bias:** The systematic deviation of the estimator's expected value from the true parameter.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, *always* write the estimator as a function of the sample, e.g., $\hat{\theta} = \hat{\theta}(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$, to score the "definition" mark.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Picture: Methods of Point Estimation

The three classical methods mandated by the KTU PCCBT503 Module 1 syllabus are:

1. **Method of Moments (MOM / MME)** — equate sample moments to population moments.
2. **Method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — maximize the probability of observing the data.
3. **Method of Least Squares (LSE)** — minimize the sum of squared deviations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Methods?**
> - MOM is the *easiest* (plug-and-chug) but often less efficient.
> - MLE is the *gold standard* — asymptotically unbiased, efficient, and normally distributed.
> - LSE is dominant in *regression models* (covered in detail in Module 2).

---

## 2.2 Method of Moments (MME)

### Logic Steps
- **Step 1:** Compute the $k$-th *population* moment about the origin:
$$
\mu_k' = E(X^k) = \text{function of }\theta
$$
- **Step 2:** Compute the $k$-th *sample* moment about the origin:
$$
m_k' = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i^k
$$
- **Step 3:** Equate $\mu_k' = m_k'$ for as many $k$ as the number of unknown parameters.
- **Step 4:** Solve the resulting system of equations for the parameters.

### Worked Skeleton (one parameter)
$$
E(X) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \hat{\theta}_{MME} \text{ is the solution.}
$$

---

## 2.3 Method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

### Logic Steps
- **Step 1:** Write the **likelihood function** $L(\theta)$ — the joint pmf/pdf of the sample, viewed as a function of $\theta$.
- **Step 2:** Take the natural log to obtain the **log-likelihood** $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta)$.
- **Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$ and set equal to zero (the **likelihood equation**):
$$
\frac{d\ell(\theta)}{d\theta} = 0
$$
- **Step 4:** Solve for $\theta$; verify it is a *maximum* using the second-derivative test:
$$
\frac{d^2\ell(\theta)}{d\theta^2}\bigg|_{\theta=\hat{\theta}} < 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic Trick:** Since $L(\theta) = \prod f(x_i;\theta)$ contains products, taking the log converts them to sums, making differentiation vastly easier. This single step is worth **½ to 1 mark** in KTU valuations.

---

## 2.4 Properties of Good Estimators (KTU-Favorite Theory Topic)

| Property | Formal Definition | Test Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbiasedness** | $E(\hat{\theta}) = \theta$ | Bias $B(\hat{\theta}) = E(\hat{\theta}) - \theta = 0$ |
| **Consistency** | $\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $n \to \infty$ | $\lim_{n\to\infty} E(\hat{\theta}_n) = \theta$ **and** $\lim_{n\to\infty} V(\hat{\theta}_n) = 0$ |
| **Efficiency** | Smallest variance among unbiased estimators | $V(\hat{\theta}) \le V(\tilde{\theta})$ for all unbiased $\tilde{\theta}$ |
| **Sufficiency** | Uses *all* information about $\theta$ in the sample | Neyman-Fisher Factorization Theorem applies |
| **Asymptotic Normality** | Distribution tends to Normal for large $n$ | $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}-\theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \frac{1}{I(\theta)})$ |

### 2.4.1 Bias of an Estimator
$$
\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E(\hat{\theta}) - \theta
$$
The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** combines both bias and variance:
$$
\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = V(\hat{\theta}) + [\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})]^2
$$

### 2.4.2 Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) — Efficiency Yardstick
For an unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$:
$$
V(\hat{\theta}) \ge \frac{1}{n \cdot I(\theta)}
$$
where the **Fisher Information** for one observation is:
$$
I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]
$$
An estimator achieving the CRLB is called **efficient**.

---

## 2.5 Confidence Interval Estimation

For a normal population with known variance $\sigma^2$:
$$
\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$
For a normal population with **unknown** variance (replace $\sigma$ by sample SD $S$ and use $t$):
$$
\bar{X} \pm t_{(n-1, \alpha/2)} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}
$$
For a population proportion $p$:
$$
\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample mean | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ |
| 2 | Sample variance | $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
| 3 | Likelihood function | $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i;\theta)$ |
| 4 | Log-likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum \ln f(x_i;\theta)$ |
| 5 | Score function | $U(\theta) = \dfrac{d\ell}{d\theta}$ |
| 6 | Fisher Information (one obs) | $I(\theta) = E\!\left[\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$ |
| 7 | CRLB | $V(\hat{\theta}) \ge \dfrac{1}{n I(\theta)}$ |
| 8 | Bias | $B(\hat{\theta}) = E(\hat{\theta}) - \theta$ |
| 9 | MSE | $V(\hat{\theta}) + B^2$ |
| 10 | CI for $\mu$ (known $\sigma$) | $\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 11 | CI for $\mu$ (unknown $\sigma$) | $\bar{X} \pm t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 12 | CI for proportion | $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Quality Control (Manufacturing):** Estimate the *mean lifetime* of a batch of bearings using MLE on Weibull-distributed failure times.
> - **Communication Systems:** Estimate the *signal-to-noise ratio* parameter $\theta$ from a noisy received sample.
> - **Machine Learning:** The *weights* of a linear regression model are estimated by the **Method of Least Squares** (Module 2).
> - **Bio-statistics / Public Health:** Estimate the *prevalence* of a disease using $\hat{p}$ from a random sample survey.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 MME Derivation for a Two-Parameter Distribution (Normal)

**Setup:** $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, both unknown. We need estimators $\hat{\mu}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2$.

**Step 1 — First population moment:**
$$
\mu_1' = E(X) = \mu
$$
**Step 2 — Second population moment:**
$$
\mu_2' = E(X^2) = V(X) + [E(X)]^2 = \sigma^2 + \mu^2
$$
**Step 3 — First sample moment:**
$$
m_1' = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = \bar{X}
$$
**Step 4 — Second sample moment:**
$$
m_2' = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i^2
$$
**Step 5 — Equate moments:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1' &= m_1' \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mu = \bar{X} \\
\mu_2' &= m_2' \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sigma^2 + \mu^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2
\end{aligned}
$$
**Step 6 — Solve for $\sigma^2$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2 - \hat{\mu}^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2 - \bar{X}^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2
\end{aligned}
$$
> **Conclusion:** $\hat{\mu}_{MME} = \bar{X}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MME} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$ (the MLE uses the *same* expressions, but with divisor $n$ instead of $n-1$ for variance — a classic KTU trap question).

---

## 3.2 MLE Derivation for the Parameter of a Poisson Distribution

**Setup:** $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$ i.i.d. Estimate $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Write the pmf:**
$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$
**Step 2 — Form the likelihood function (joint pmf of the sample):**
$$
L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x_i}}{x_i!}
= \frac{e^{-n\lambda} \lambda^{\sum x_i}}{\prod x_i!}
$$
**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:**
$$
\ell(\lambda) = -n\lambda + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)\ln\lambda - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln(x_i!)
$$
> *Logic:* The last term contains no $\lambda$ and is treated as a constant during differentiation.

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $\lambda$ and set to zero:**
$$
\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = -n + \frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda} = 0
$$
**Step 5 — Solve the likelihood equation:**
$$
\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{x}
$$
**Step 6 — Second-derivative test:**
$$
\frac{d^2\ell}{d\lambda^2} = -\frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda^2} \bigg|_{\lambda = \bar{x}} = -\frac{n\bar{x}}{\bar{x}^2} = -\frac{n}{\bar{x}} < 0
$$
> **Confirmed maximum.** Hence the MLE of $\lambda$ is the sample mean $\bar{x}$.

---

## 3.3 MLE for Exponential Distribution with Parameter $\theta$

**Setup:** $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\theta)$ with pdf $f(x;\theta) = \theta e^{-\theta x}$, $x > 0$.

**Step 1 — Likelihood:**
$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \theta e^{-\theta x_i} = \theta^n e^{-\theta \sum x_i}
$$
**Step 2 — Log-likelihood:**
$$
\ell(\theta) = n\ln\theta - \theta\sum x_i
$$
**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero:**
$$
\frac{d\ell}{d\theta} = \frac{n}{\theta} - \sum x_i = 0
$$
**Step 4 — Solve:**
$$
\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \frac{n}{\sum x_i} = \frac{1}{\bar{x}}
$$
**Step 5 — Second-derivative test:**
$$
\frac{d^2\ell}{d\theta^2} = -\frac{n}{\theta^2} < 0 \quad \text{(always negative — confirmed maximum)}
$$

---

## 3.4 CRLB Derivation for the Exponential Mean

**Setup:** $X \sim \text{Exp}(\theta)$, pdf $f(x) = \theta e^{-\theta x}$.

**Step 1 — Log-pdf:**
$$
\ln f = \ln\theta - \theta x
$$
**Step 2 — First derivative (score):**
$$
\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{\theta} - x
$$
**Step 3 — Second derivative:**
$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \theta^2} = -\frac{1}{\theta^2}
$$
**Step 4 — Fisher Information for *one* observation:**
$$
I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = -E\!\left[-\frac{1}{\theta^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\theta^2}
$$
**Step 5 — CRLB for sample of size $n$:**
$$
V(\hat{\theta}) \ge \frac{1}{n I(\theta)} = \frac{\theta^2}{n}
$$
**Step 6 — Verify MLE is efficient:**
$$
V(\hat{\theta}_{MLE}) = V\!\left(\frac{1}{\bar{X}}\right) \approx \frac{\theta^2}{n} \quad \text{(for large }n\text{ by Delta method)}
$$
> **Efficiency achieved in the limit** — a hallmark property of MLE.

---

## 3.5 Confidence Interval for Mean — Detailed Build

**Step 1 — Standardize the sample mean:**
$$
Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)
$$
**Step 2 — Use the standard normal property $P(-z_{\alpha/2} \le Z \le z_{\alpha/2}) = 1 - \alpha$:**
$$
P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \le \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \le z_{\alpha/2}\right) = 1-\alpha
$$
**Step 3 — Multiply through by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:**
$$
P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \bar{X} - \mu \le z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1-\alpha
$$
**Step 4 — Subtract $\bar{X}$ and multiply by $-1$ (reverses inequalities):**
$$
P\!\left(\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \mu \le \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1-\alpha
$$
**Final 100(1−α)% CI for $\mu$:**
$$
\boxed{\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}}
$$
> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Logic Test:** If asked for 99% CI, use $z_{0.005} = 2.576$. If asked for 95% CI, use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$. Memorize these two constants — they appear in **every KTU statistics paper**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow of Parameter Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Population with unknown parameter theta] --> B[Draw random sample X1 X2 ... Xn]
    B --> C{Which Method?}
    C -->|MME| D[Equate population moments to sample moments]
    C -->|MLE| E[Form likelihood L theta then maximize]
    C -->|LSE| F[Minimize sum of squared residuals]
    D --> G[Estimate theta-hat]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Evaluate Properties]
    H --> H1[Unbiasedness]
    H --> H2[Consistency]
    H --> H3[Efficiency via CRLB]
    H --> H4[Sufficiency]
    H --> I[Construct Confidence Interval]
    I --> J[Decision or Inference]
```

## 4.2 MLE Algorithm Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with pdf or pmf f x theta] --> B[Write likelihood L theta equals product of f xi theta]
    B --> C[Apply ln to get log-likelihood ell theta]
    C --> D[Differentiate ell theta with respect to theta]
    D --> E[Solve the score equation equals zero]
    E --> F{Is solution a maximum?}
    F -->|No| G[Check boundary or use second derivative test]
    F -->|Yes| H[Accept theta-hat as MLE]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Fisher Information and CRLB]
    I --> J[Declare efficiency]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Estimator Properties (Venn-style Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All Estimators] --> B[Unbiased?]
    B -->|Yes| C[Efficient? achieves CRLB]
    B -->|No| D[Has Bias]
    C -->|Yes| E[Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator MVUE]
    C -->|No| F[Unbiased but not efficient]
    D --> G[Consistent?]
    G -->|Yes| H[Asymptotically Unbiased]
    G -->|No| I[Inconsistent — discard]
    A --> J[Sufficient?]
    J -->|Yes| K[Uses all sample information]
    J -->|No| L[Some info wasted]
```

## 4.4 Confidence Interval Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample data x1..xn] --> B[Compute point estimate e.g. x-bar]
    B --> C[Identify sampling distribution]
    C --> D[Known sigma or unknown?]
    D -->|Known| E[Use Z = x-bar - mu / sigma over sqrt n]
    D -->|Unknown| F[Use T = x-bar - mu / S over sqrt n]
    E --> G[Pick confidence level 1 minus alpha]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Find critical value z or t]
    H --> I[Compute margin of error E]
    I --> J[Form interval estimate]
```
