# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Fisher Linear Discriminant. State the Fisher criterion and explain what it seeks to optimise.**

**Model Answer:**

The Fisher Linear Discriminant is a projection technique that maps a $d$-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ onto a one-dimensional line such that the resulting scalar values are maximally separated between classes.

The **Fisher criterion** is the Rayleigh quotient:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$$

- The **numerator** $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}$ measures the **between-class variance** after projection.
- The **denominator** $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$ measures the **within-class variance** after projection.

The optimal $\mathbf{w}^*$ maximises $J$, i.e., **simultaneously maximises between-class separation and minimises within-class scatter**, producing the direction that best discriminates the classes along a single line.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between within-class scatter matrix ($\mathbf{S}_W$) and between-class scatter matrix ($\mathbf{S}_B$) with their formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $\mathbf{S}_W$ (Within-class) | $\mathbf{S}_B$ (Between-class) |
|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Spread of samples *around their own class mean* | Spread of *class means* around the global mean |
| **Formula** | $\sum_i \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top$ | $\sum_i n_i(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$ |
| **Ideal value** | Small (compact classes) | Large (well-separated means) |
| **Rank** | At most $N - c$ | At most $c - 1$ |
| **Total scatter relation** | $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$ | Forms complementary part |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $\mathbf{S}_W$ uses $N$ summations (one per sample), while $\mathbf{S}_B$ uses only $c$ (one per class). Hence $\mathbf{S}_B$ is dramatically lower-rank.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Consider the following two-class dataset in $\mathbb{R}^2$:**

| Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 4 | 2 | $C_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 2 | 4 | $C_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 3 | 3 | $C_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_4$ | 8 | 6 | $C_2$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_5$ | 6 | 8 | $C_2$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_6$ | 7 | 7 | $C_2$ |

**(a)** Compute the class means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2$, the within-class scatter matrix $\mathbf{S}_W$, and the between-class scatter matrix $\mathbf{S}_B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the optimal Fisher projection vector $\mathbf{w}^*$, determine the decision boundary, and classify the test point $\mathbf{x}_{\text{test}} = (5, 5)$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a) — Scatter Matrices**

**Step 1 — Class means.**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix}4+2+3 \\ 2+4+3\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}3 \\ 3\end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix}8+6+7 \\ 6+8+7\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}7 \\ 7\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Within-class scatter for $C_1$.**

Centred vectors for $C_1$: $(1,-1)^\top, (-1,1)^\top, (0,0)^\top$.

$$\mathbf{S}_{W,1} = \begin{pmatrix}1+1+0 & -1-1+0 \\ -1-1+0 & 1+1+0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Within-class scatter for $C_2$.**

Centred vectors for $C_2$: $(1,-1)^\top, (-1,1)^\top, (0,0)^\top$.

$$\mathbf{S}_{W,2} = \begin{pmatrix}2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Total within-class scatter.**

$$\mathbf{S}_W = \mathbf{S}_{W,1} + \mathbf{S}_{W,2} = \begin{pmatrix}4 & -4 \\ -4 & 4\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Global mean.**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix}10 \\ 10\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}5 \\ 5\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Between-class scatter.**

$$\mathbf{S}_B = 3(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top + 3(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$$

$$= 3\begin{pmatrix}-2 \\ -2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}-2 & -2\end{pmatrix} + 3\begin{pmatrix}2 \\ 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}2 & 2\end{pmatrix} = 3\begin{pmatrix}4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4\end{pmatrix} + 3\begin{pmatrix}4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{S}_B = \begin{pmatrix}24 & 24 \\ 24 & 24\end{pmatrix}$$

(Alternatively, $\mathbf{S}_B = \frac{n_1 n_2}{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^\top = \frac{9}{6}\begin{pmatrix}-4 & -4 \\ -4 & -4\end{pmatrix}\cdot 2 = \begin{pmatrix}24 & 24 \\ 24 & 24\end{pmatrix}$.)

#### **Solution to (b) — Projection and Classification**

**Step 1 — Inverse of $\mathbf{S}_W$.**

$$\det(\mathbf{S}_W) = 16 - 16 = 0 \quad \text{— SINGULAR!}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The matrix $\mathbf{S}_W$ is **singular** here (it is rank-1). Direct inversion is impossible. The proper resolution is to add a **regularisation term** $\epsilon \mathbf{I}$ (a Tikhonov regularisation), called *Pseudo-inverse LDA* or *Regularised Discriminant Analysis (RDA)*.

**Step 2 — Apply regularisation.** Take $\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}} = \mathbf{S}_W + 0.1 \mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}} = \begin{pmatrix}4.1 & -4 \\ -4 & 4.1\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}}) = (4.1)^2 - 16 = 16.81 - 16 = 0.81$$

$$(\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}})^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix}4.1 & 4 \\ 4 & 4.1\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{w}^*$.**

$$\mathbf{w}^* = (\mathbf{S}_W^{\text{reg}})^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix}4.1 & 4 \\ 4 & 4.1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}-4 \\ -4\end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix}-16.4 - 16 \\ -16 - 16.4\end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.81}\begin{pmatrix}-32.4 \\ -32.4\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-40 \\ -40\end{pmatrix}$$

The direction is along $(-1, -1)$, i.e., the line $x_1 = x_2$.

**Step 4 — Projected means.**

$$m_1 = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (-40)(3) + (-40)(3) = -240$$

$$m_2 = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (-40)(7) + (-40)(7) = -560$$

**Step 5 — Decision threshold.**

$$y_0 = \frac{m_1 + m_2}{2} = \frac{-240 - 560}{2} = -400$$

**Step 6 — Classify $\mathbf{x}_{\text{test}} = (5, 5)$.**

$$y_{\text{test}} = (-40)(5) + (-40)(5) = -400$$

Since $y_{\text{test}} = y_0 = -400$, the test point lies **exactly on the decision boundary**. By convention (or by tie-breaking rule), we assign to $C_1$ (or note the ambiguous classification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric verification:** The boundary $x_1 = x_2$ is *exactly* the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (3,3)$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (7,7)$, which is the **Bayes-optimal boundary** for this symmetric dataset.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and prove the identity $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$. Explain the significance of this decomposition in the context of Linear Discriminant Analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-class problem with common covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, derive the Bayes-optimal linear discriminant functions $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ and show that the decision boundary between any two classes $C_i$ and $C_j$ is linear. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a) — Proof of $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$**

**Step 1 — Recall the definitions.**

$$\mathbf{S}_T = \sum_{i=1}^{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$$

$$\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top$$

$$\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$$

**Step 2 — Introduce a strategic decomposition.** Add and subtract the class mean inside the total scatter:

$$(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = (\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

**Step 3 — Square the decomposition.**

$$(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top = (\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top + (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top + \text{cross terms}$$

The cross terms are:

$$(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top + (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top$$

**Step 4 — Sum over class $C_i$.** Summing over all $j = 1, \ldots, n_i$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_{ij} - n_i \boldsymbol{\mu}_i = n_i \boldsymbol{\mu}_i - n_i \boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \mathbf{0}$$

So the cross terms **vanish** when summed over a class.

**Step 5 — Aggregate over all classes.**

$$\mathbf{S}_T = \sum_{i=1}^{c}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x}_{ij} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top + \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Significance in LDA:**

This decomposition reveals a beautiful geometric truth: the total variability in the data is partitioned into **two orthogonal sources of information** — variability *within* classes and variability *between* classes. Fisher's criterion elegantly exploits this by projecting out the *useful* between-class signal while suppressing the *nuisance* within-class noise.

#### **Solution to (b) — Bayes-Optimal Multi-Class LDF**

**Step 1 — Bayes' theorem and log-posterior.** For class $C_i$ with prior $P(C_i) = \pi_i$ and class-conditional Gaussian density $f_i(\mathbf{x})$ (common $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$):

$$P(C_i \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \pi_i \cdot f_i(\mathbf{x})$$

The decision rule assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the **largest log-posterior**:

$$\delta_i(\mathbf{x}) = \log \pi_i + \log f_i(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2 — Expand the log-Gaussian.**

$$\log f_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{d}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\log\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the quadratic term.**

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i$$

**Step 4 — Form the discriminant function.** Combining with the prior and dropping the term $-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ (which is **identical for all classes** and thus irrelevant):

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \log \pi_i$$

$$\boxed{g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}}$$

where $\mathbf{w}_i = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and $w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \log \pi_i$.

**Step 5 — Show linearity of pairwise boundary.** The decision boundary between $C_i$ and $C_j$ is the set of $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$:

$$\mathbf{w}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + w_{i0} = \mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x} + w_{j0}$$

$$(\mathbf{w}_i - \mathbf{w}_j)^\top \mathbf{x} = w_{j0} - w_{i0}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{a}^\top \mathbf{x} = b}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{a} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j), \quad b = \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^\top\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^\top\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j - \log(\pi_i/\pi_j)$$

This is a **linear equation in $\mathbf{x}$** — a hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^d$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop** the common term $-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Inverting $\mathbf{S}_W$ without checking singularity** when samples are insufficient or features are linearly dependent.
> 3. **Confusing $\mathbf{S}_W$ with $\mathbf{S}_B$** in the Rayleigh quotient (the numerator is between-class, denominator is within-class — many students reverse these).
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of common covariance** when deriving the Bayes LDF.
> 5. **Forgetting to normalise** the discriminant projection — $\mathbf{w}$ can be scaled arbitrarily, but the threshold $y_0$ depends on the chosen scale.
> 6. **Skipping the verification** that $\mathbf{S}_B$ is rank-$(c-1)$ in the multi-class case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Linear Discriminant Function Analysis**

- ✅ **LDA is supervised** — requires labelled training data; unsupervised counterpart is **PCA**.
- ✅ The **Fisher criterion** $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ is the heart of LDA — maximising this Rayleigh quotient yields the optimal projection.
- ✅ The **two-class optimal direction** is $\mathbf{w}^* \propto \mathbf{S}_W^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$.
- ✅ The **generalised eigenproblem** $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}$ provides eigenvectors for the multi-class case.
- ✅ The number of **non-zero eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{S}_W^{-1}$ is at most $c - 1$ ⟹ at most $c-1$ useful discriminant functions.
- ✅ The **scatter identity** $\mathbf{S}_T = \mathbf{S}_W + \mathbf{S}_B$ is a high-yield derivation in KTU exams.
- ✅ The **Bayes LDF** under equal covariance and Gaussian assumption: $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$.
- ✅ **Decision threshold** for two-class equal-prior case: $y_0 = \frac{m_1 + m_2}{2} = \mathbf{w}^\top \frac{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\mu}_2}{2}$.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis distance** is the Bayes-optimal distance metric when classes share covariance.
- ✅ **Assumptions of LDA**: (1) Multivariate normality of class-conditional distributions, (2) Equal covariance matrices across classes, (3) Independence of features (for naive Bayes variant).
- ✅ **Regularisation**: When $\mathbf{S}_W$ is singular, add $\epsilon \mathbf{I}$ (RDA) or use PCA pre-processing.
- ✅ **KTU Common Mistake**: Confusing LDA with **logistic regression** — LDA is *generative* (models $P(\mathbf{x}\mid C_i)$) while logistic regression is *discriminative* (models $P(C_i\mid \mathbf{x})$ directly).
- ✅ **Performance metric**: Use **confusion matrix**, **accuracy**, **precision**, **recall**, **F1-score** to evaluate LDA classifiers.
- ✅ **Practical software note**: In R, the function `lda()` from the `MASS` package performs LDA; in Python, `sklearn.discriminant_analysis.LinearDiscriminantAnalysis`.
- ✅ **Multi-class extension**: For $c > 2$ classes, **at most** $\min(c-1, d)$ discriminant functions are extracted.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Linear Discriminant Functions and Their Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT503 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimating Linear Discriminant Functions and Their Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Linear Discriminant Function** is a mathematical function used in pattern classification to assign an input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ to one of $c$ classes $\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c$ by computing a scalar (or vector) score and selecting the class with the highest value.

For a **two-class** problem, a linear discriminant function takes the canonical form:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_d)^T$ is the **weight vector** (or coefficient vector).
- $w_0$ is the **bias term** (also called threshold).
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)^T$ is the input feature vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (Two-Class):**
> Decide $\omega_1$ if $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, otherwise decide $\omega_2$.
> The equation $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ defines the **decision hyperplane** in $d$-dimensional feature space.

For a **multiclass** problem (with $c$ classes), we define $c$ linear discriminant functions:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, c$$

The classification rule is: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$. Ties are resolved arbitrarily.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Spam Filter" Analogy:**
> Imagine you sort incoming emails into "Important" and "Spam" using a checklist. You assign points (+1) for every "URGENT" keyword, (-2) for every "FREE OFFER", and (+0.5) if the sender is in your contact list. The final score is a *weighted sum* of these signals plus a baseline threshold. If the final score is positive, you label it "Important"; otherwise "Spam." This score is essentially a **linear discriminant function** — a hyperplane (a single line in 2D) cleanly separates the two categories.

In $d$-dimensional feature space, this separating boundary becomes a **hyperplane** (a line in 2D, a plane in 3D, an abstract flat surface in higher dimensions). The vector $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to this hyperplane, and $w_0$ controls how far the hyperplane is from the origin.

## 1.3 Why Linear Discriminant Functions?

- **Computational simplicity:** Only requires matrix-vector products.
- **Theoretical foundation:** Can be derived directly from the Gaussian (normal) class-conditional density assumption.
- **Optimality under specific conditions:** They are the *optimal* Bayesian classifiers when features are Gaussian with shared or identity covariance.
- **Generalizability:** They form the building block for the Perceptron, Logistic Regression, Linear SVM, and LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis).

## 1.4 Augmented Feature Vector Form

For analytical convenience, the bias $w_0$ is absorbed into the weight vector by augmenting the input:

$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{w} \\ w_0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then the discriminant function simplifies to:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$$

This compact form is heavily used in the **Perceptron** and **MSE (Widrow-Hoff)** derivations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Interpretation of the Hyperplane

The decision boundary $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ is a hyperplane with the following geometric properties:

1. **Normal vector:** $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to the hyperplane.
2. **Distance from origin:** The signed distance from the origin to the hyperplane is $\dfrac{w_0}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$.
3. **Distance of any point:** The signed distance from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane is $\dfrac{g(\mathbf{x}_0)}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$.

These properties are obtained by projecting the point onto the normal direction of the hyperplane.

## 2.2 Discriminant Functions Derived from Gaussian Densities

Assume the class-conditional density is multivariate Gaussian: $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i)$.

The **Bayes optimal discriminant** is:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

### Case 1: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$ (Spherical, Identical Covariance)

The covariance is a scalar multiple of the identity matrix. The quadratic terms cancel out, leaving a **linear** discriminant function:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$$

with the parameters:

$$\mathbf{w}_i = \frac{\boldsymbol{\mu}_i}{\sigma^2}, \qquad w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T \boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The decision boundary between $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is a **hyperplane perpendicular to the line connecting $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$**, and it lies exactly at the **midpoint** of that line (when priors are equal).

### Case 2: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (General, Identical Covariance)

All classes share the same covariance matrix. Again, quadratic terms cancel:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$$

with:

$$\mathbf{w}_i = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \qquad w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

The decision boundary $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ is:

$$\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0) = 0$$

where $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)$ and $\mathbf{x}_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) - \dfrac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(\mathbf{w}_i - \mathbf{w}_j)}{2 \|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)\|^2}$ (the midpoint correction due to differing biases).

## 2.3 The Perceptron Criterion

The perceptron algorithm is an **error-correcting** iterative procedure for finding a weight vector $\mathbf{a}$ that classifies training samples correctly. Define the perceptron criterion:

$$J_p(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}} (-\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y})$$

where $\mathcal{Y}$ is the set of training samples **misclassified** under the current $\mathbf{a}$. The gradient descent update rule is:

$$\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k) \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}_k} \mathbf{y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Theorem (Rosenblatt, Nilsson):** If the training samples are **linearly separable**, the perceptron algorithm is guaranteed to find a separating hyperplane in a finite number of steps. If **not separable**, the algorithm oscillates indefinitely.

## 2.4 The Minimum Squared Error (MSE) Procedure

To avoid oscillation on non-separable data, we can pose classification as solving a **linear system**:

$$\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{b}$$

where $\mathbf{Y}$ is the $(n \times (d+1))$ matrix of augmented training samples and $\mathbf{b}$ is a vector of $+1$'s and $-1$'s encoding the desired class. The **least-squares** solution (the Widrow-Hoff / Ho-Kashyap connection) is:

$$\mathbf{a} = (\mathbf{Y}^T \mathbf{Y})^{-1}\mathbf{Y}^T \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{Y}^{\dagger}\mathbf{b}$$

where $\mathbf{Y}^{\dagger}$ is the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse**. This works even when classes are not linearly separable.

## 2.5 Multiclass Linear Machines (ω-Matrix)

For $c$ classes, we can pack all $c$ weight vectors into a single weight matrix:

$$\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1 \; \mathbf{w}_2 \; \dots \; \mathbf{w}_c] \quad (d \times c)$$

The discriminant is the vector:

$$\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{w}_0$$

We assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$. A **linear machine** refers to any classifier with this structure (not just the Bayesian optimal one).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are **high-yield** for KTU University Exams. Memorize and practice them.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Parameters / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Two-class linear discriminant | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$ | $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $w_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Augmented form | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{a} = [\mathbf{w}; w_0]$, $\mathbf{y} = [\mathbf{x}; 1]$ |
| Distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to hyperplane | $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$ | Signed scalar distance |
| Normal vector direction | $\mathbf{w}$ | Perpendicular to decision boundary |
| Bayes optimal (equal covariance) | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} - \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | Spherical: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$ |
| Perceptron criterion | $J_p(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}}(-\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y})$ | $\mathcal{Y}$ is misclassified set |
| Perceptron update | $\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k)\sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}_k}\mathbf{y}$ | $\eta(k) > 0$ is learning rate |
| MSE solution | $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{Y}^{\dagger}\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{Y}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y})^{-1}\mathbf{Y}^T$ |
| Decision rule (multi-class) | $\omega_i = \arg\max_i \mathbf{w}_i^T\mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$ | Pick class with max score |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Linear Discriminants

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Biomedical Engineering | ECG/EEG signal classification (normal vs arrhythmia) |
| Civil Engineering | Structural damage detection from vibration signatures |
| Computer Vision | Face recognition, OCR character recognition |
| Finance | Credit-card fraud detection, credit scoring |
| Industry Quality Control | Defect detection on assembly line sensor data |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discriminant Function for $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$

We start from Bayes' rule. The optimal class is the one maximizing the posterior:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

Hence, a monotonic function of the posterior is acceptable as a discriminant. Define:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

For a Gaussian with covariance $\sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{d/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)\right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\right]$$

The term $-\tfrac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2)$ does not depend on $i$ and is dropped:

\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) &= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\right] + \ln P(\omega_i) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}

The common term $-\tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x}$ cancels across all classes. What remains is the **linear** discriminant:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$$

where:

$$\mathbf{w}_i = \frac{\boldsymbol{\mu}_i}{\sigma^2}, \qquad w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Decision Boundary for Two Gaussians with $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$

Set $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for boundary. Using the general-covariance form:

\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) - g_j(\mathbf{x}) &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + w_0 &= 0
\end{aligned}

where:

$$\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)$$

The bias $w_0$ is computed by expanding both discriminants and collecting constant terms:

$$w_0 = -\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) + \ln\frac{P(\omega_i)}{P(\omega_j)}$$

To find the **midpoint** $\mathbf{x}_0$ of the hyperplane (the point on the hyperplane closest to both means), set $\mathbf{x}_0$ such that it lies halfway along the line connecting the means in the Mahalanobis-metric sense:

$$\mathbf{x}_0 = \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) - \frac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_0^*}{\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{w}}\mathbf{w}$$

where $\mathbf{w}_0^* = \ln P(\omega_i) - \ln P(\omega_j)$. When priors are equal, $\mathbf{w}_0^* = 0$ and the hyperplane passes exactly through the midpoint $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)/2$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Perceptron Criterion and Its Gradient

The perceptron criterion is a **sum over misclassified samples**:

$$J_p(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}} (-\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y})$$

A sample $\mathbf{y}$ is misclassified by the perceptron if:

- It belongs to $\omega_1$ (desired label $+1$) but $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} \leq 0$, **or**
- It belongs to $\omega_2$ (desired label $-1$) but $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} \geq 0$.

When samples are **linearly separable**, there exists an $\mathbf{a}^*$ such that $\mathbf{a}^{*T}\mathbf{y} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}_1$ and $\mathbf{a}^{*T}\mathbf{y} < 0$ for all $\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}_2$. With the label-flipping trick (replacing samples of $\omega_2$ with their negatives), all relevant samples satisfy $\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{y} < 0$, hence $- \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} > 0$ for misclassified samples, and the criterion is positive.

The gradient of $J_p$ with respect to $\mathbf{a}$ is:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{a}} J_p = \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}} (-\mathbf{y})$$

So the **gradient descent** update is:

$$\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) - \eta(k) \nabla_{\mathbf{a}} J_p = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k) \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}_k} \mathbf{y}$$

This is the **batch perceptron rule**. For the **single-sample (on-line) perceptron rule**, when one sample $\mathbf{y}_k$ is misclassified:

$$\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k)\mathbf{y}_k$$

## 3.4 Derivation: MSE Procedure (Widrow-Hoff)

We seek the weight vector $\mathbf{a}$ that minimizes:

$$J_s(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}_i - b_i)^2 = \|\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b}\|^2$$

Setting the gradient to zero:

\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{a}} J_s &= 2\mathbf{Y}^T(\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b}) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{a} &= \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{b} \\
\Rightarrow \;\; \mathbf{a} &= (\mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y})^{-1}\mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}

provided $\mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y}$ is non-singular. The matrix $(\mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y})^{-1}\mathbf{Y}^T$ is the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{Y}^{\dagger}$ of $\mathbf{Y}$ (when $\mathbf{Y}$ has full column rank). If $\mathbf{Y}$ is rank-deficient, the **pseudoinverse** is the unique solution of minimum norm that minimizes $\|\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b}\|$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MSE ≠ Bayes Optimal:** The MSE solution minimizes squared error, not classification error. It is not equivalent to the optimal Bayesian discriminant, but it is closely related: when classes are Gaussian with equal covariance, the MSE solution lies along the same direction as the Bayes optimal $\mathbf{w}$ (a property known as *Fisher's linear discriminant* direction).

## 3.5 Worked Example (Model Solution)

> **Problem:** Two Gaussian classes with $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1, 0)^T$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (-1, 0)^T$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_1 = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_2 = \mathbf{I}$, and equal priors $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2) = 0.5$. Find the linear discriminant and the decision boundary.

**Solution:**

For $\sigma^2 = 1$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{w}_1 = \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1, 0)^T, \quad w_{10} = -\tfrac{1}{2}(1, 0)\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix} + \ln(0.5) = -0.5 - \ln 2$$

$$\mathbf{w}_2 = \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (-1, 0)^T, \quad w_{20} = -\tfrac{1}{2}(-1, 0)\begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\end{pmatrix} + \ln(0.5) = -0.5 - \ln 2$$

Setting $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = g_2(\mathbf{x})$:

$$x_1 - 0.5 - \ln 2 = -x_1 - 0.5 - \ln 2$$

$$2x_1 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 = 0$$

The decision boundary is the **vertical line $x_1 = 0$**, the perpendicular bisector of the line joining the two means — exactly as expected.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Linear Classification Pipeline

This block diagram describes the workflow of a linear discriminant function in a classification system. It maps the functional flow from input features to class assignment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector X of dimension d] --> B[Augment with bias unit y equals X followed by 1]
    B --> C[Multiply with weight vector a of size d plus 1]
    C --> D[Compute Discriminant Score a transpose y]
    D --> E{Compare with class scores}
    E --> F[Class 1 score g1 of X]
    E --> G[Class 2 score g2 of X]
    E --> H[Class c score gc of X]
    F --> I[Argmax Selection]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Class Label omega i]
    B --> K[Training Phase Estimate Parameters]
    K --> L[Bayes Optimal use Gaussian density]
    K --> M[Perceptron Criterion minimize Jp]
    K --> N[MSE Procedure minimize sum of squared errors]
    L --> C
    M --> C
    N --> C
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Geometric Visualization: Two-Class Decision Boundary

A schematic Mermaid flow showing how the hyperplane separates the feature space.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Class omega1 region plus side of hyperplane] --- H[Decision Hyperplane w transpose X plus w0 equals 0]
    H --- P2[Class omega2 region minus side of hyperplane]
    H --> N[Normal vector w points from omega2 to omega1]
    X[Arbitrary point X0 in space] --> D[Compute signed distance gX0 divided by norm w]
    D --> S{Positive or Negative}
    S -->|Positive| P1
    S -->|Negative| P2
    style H fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:3px
    style P1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Decision Tree for Estimating the Discriminant Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start with classification problem] --> Q1{Are class conditional densities known?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Gaussian assumed| Q2{Is covariance identical and spherical sigma squared I?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is data linearly separable?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Bayes1[Use Bayes Optimal Formula wi equals mui over sigma squared]
    Q2 -->|No identical general Sigma| Bayes2[Use Bayes Optimal Formula wi equals Sigma inverse mui]
    Bayes1 --> STOP[Final Discriminant Function]
    Bayes2 --> STOP
    Q3 -->|Yes| Perc[Use Perceptron Criterion Jp]
    Q3 -->|No| MSE[Use MSE Procedure Pseudoinverse]
    Perc --> STOP
    MSE --> STOP
    style S fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style STOP fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:3px
    style Perc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style MSE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When a problem statement says "Gaussian, equal covariance, equal priors" — that triggers **Case 1 / Case 2** of the Bayes optimal formulas. When it says "find a weight vector that classifies these samples" without distributional assumptions — that triggers the **Perceptron** or **MSE** route.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a linear discriminant function for a two-class problem and explain the meaning of the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ and the bias $w_0$.**

**Model Answer:**
A linear discriminant function for a two-class problem is defined as $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$, where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the input feature vector, $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector, and $w_0$ is the bias. The decision rule is: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_1$ if $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ and to $\omega_2$ otherwise. The weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is **normal (perpendicular) to the decision hyperplane** and points towards the side of $\omega_1$. The bias $w_0$ controls the **position of the hyperplane relative to the origin**; specifically, the signed distance of the hyperplane from the origin is $w_0 / \|\mathbf{w}\|$.

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**Q2. State the Bayes optimal linear discriminant function when the class-conditional densities are Gaussian with identical covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. Write down the form of the decision boundary.**

**Model Answer:**
The Bayes optimal discriminant function for class $\omega_i$ under multivariate Gaussian densities with shared covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T \mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

This is linear in $\mathbf{x}$ because the quadratic term $\mathbf{x}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ is the same for all classes and cancels out. The decision boundary between classes $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is obtained by setting $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$, which yields a hyperplane of the form $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ with $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to drop the common term $-\tfrac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2)$ and the term $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x}/(2\sigma^2)$ when deriving the linear discriminant — leading to a "quadratic" expression where it should be linear. **Penalty: Up to 3 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the perceptron criterion $J_p$ (which is a sum of negative products) with the MSE criterion $J_s$ (which is a sum of squared residuals). The two are **not interchangeable** — perceptron handles separable data; MSE handles general data. **Penalty: Up to 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ (a scalar) as a column vector instead of a scalar. Use **dot product** notation. **Penalty: 1 mark for sloppy notation.**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Adapted for Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the Bayes optimal linear discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ when the class-conditional densities are multivariate Gaussian with covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$ (i.e., identical spherical covariance). Clearly state the resulting expressions for the weight vector $\mathbf{w}_i$ and the bias $w_{i0}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Given $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (2, 2)^T$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (0, 0)^T$, $\sigma^2 = 1$, and equal priors, determine the decision boundary and the discriminant functions for the two classes. Comment on the geometric orientation of the hyperplane with respect to the means. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Starting from the Bayes discriminant:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Substituting the spherical Gaussian density:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{d/2}}\exp\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\|^2\right)$$

Taking the logarithm and expanding the squared norm:

\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) &= -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\right] + \ln P(\omega_i) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}

The term $-\tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x}$ is **independent of $i$** and is dropped (it cancels in all comparisons). We obtain the **linear discriminant function**:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$$

where:

$$\mathbf{w}_i = \frac{\boldsymbol{\mu}_i}{\sigma^2}, \qquad w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Substituting the given values:

$$\mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{1}\begin{pmatrix}2\\2\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\2\end{pmatrix}, \quad w_{10} = -\frac{1}{2}\left[(2)(2)+(2)(2)\right] + \ln(0.5) = -4 - \ln 2$$

$$\mathbf{w}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}0\\0\end{pmatrix}, \quad w_{20} = 0 + \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

The discriminant functions are:

$$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = 2x_1 + 2x_2 - 4 - \ln 2, \qquad g_2(\mathbf{x}) = -\ln 2$$

Setting $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = g_2(\mathbf{x})$:

$$2x_1 + 2x_2 - 4 - \ln 2 = -\ln 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 + x_2 = 2$$

The decision boundary is the **line $x_1 + x_2 = 2$** in 2D feature space. This line is **perpendicular to the line connecting $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (2,2)^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (0,0)^T$** (whose direction is $(1,1)^T$, parallel to the normal of the hyperplane). With equal priors, the hyperplane passes through the midpoint $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)/2 = (1,1)^T$, and indeed $(1)+(1) = 2$. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Adapted]**

**(a)** State the perceptron criterion $J_p(\mathbf{a})$ for a two-class problem. Derive the gradient-descent update rule for the weight vector $\mathbf{a}$. State the perceptron convergence theorem and one of its practical limitations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider the following 4 two-dimensional training samples (already label-flipped so that all true class labels are $+1$):
$\mathbf{y}_1 = (1, 0, 1)^T$, $\mathbf{y}_2 = (0, 1, 1)^T$, $\mathbf{y}_3 = (-1, 0, 1)^T$, $\mathbf{y}_4 = (0, -1, 1)^T$. Starting with $\mathbf{a}(0) = (0, 0, 0)^T$ and learning rate $\eta = 1$, perform two iterations of the on-line perceptron algorithm. Show the weight vector after each update. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The perceptron criterion is defined as the sum of negative products over the misclassified set $\mathcal{Y}$:

$$J_p(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}} (-\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y})$$

A sample is misclassified if $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} \leq 0$ (after the standard sign convention). The gradient is:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{a}} J_p(\mathbf{a}) = -\sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}} \mathbf{y}$$

Applying gradient descent with step size $\eta(k)$:

$$\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) - \eta(k) \nabla_{\mathbf{a}} J_p = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k) \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}_k} \mathbf{y}$$

For the **on-line (single-sample) variant**:

$$\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k)\mathbf{y}_k \quad \text{when } \mathbf{a}(k)^T \mathbf{y}_k \leq 0$$

**Convergence Theorem:** If the training samples are linearly separable, the perceptron algorithm is guaranteed to find a separating weight vector in a finite number of steps.

**Practical Limitation:** If the data is **not linearly separable**, the algorithm oscillates and never converges.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Iteration 1, Step 1:** $\mathbf{a}(0) = (0,0,0)^T$. Test $\mathbf{y}_1 = (1,0,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}(0)^T \mathbf{y}_1 = 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1 = 0 \leq 0$ → **misclassified**.
Update: $\mathbf{a}(1) = \mathbf{a}(0) + 1 \cdot \mathbf{y}_1 = (1, 0, 1)^T$.

**Iteration 1, Step 2:** Test $\mathbf{y}_2 = (0,1,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}(1)^T \mathbf{y}_2 = (1)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(1) = 1 > 0$ → **correctly classified**, no update.

**Iteration 1, Step 3:** Test $\mathbf{y}_3 = (-1,0,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}(1)^T \mathbf{y}_3 = (1)(-1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) = 0 \leq 0$ → **misclassified**.
Update: $\mathbf{a}(2) = \mathbf{a}(1) + 1 \cdot \mathbf{y}_3 = (0, 0, 2)^T$.

**Iteration 1, Step 4:** Test $\mathbf{y}_4 = (0,-1,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}(2)^T \mathbf{y}_4 = (0)(0) + (0)(-1) + (2)(1) = 2 > 0$ → **correctly classified**, no update.

End of Iteration 1: $\mathbf{a} = (0, 0, 2)^T$.

**Iteration 2, Step 1:** Test $\mathbf{y}_1 = (1,0,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}_1 = 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2 > 0$ → **correct**, no update.

**Iteration 2, Step 2:** Test $\mathbf{y}_2 = (0,1,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}_2 = 0 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2 > 0$ → **correct**, no update.

**Iteration 2, Step 3:** Test $\mathbf{y}_3 = (-1,0,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}_3 = 0(-1) + 0(0) + 2(1) = 2 > 0$ → **correct**, no update.

**Iteration 2, Step 4:** Test $\mathbf{y}_4 = (0,-1,1)^T$:
$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}_4 = 0(0) + 0(-1) + 2(1) = 2 > 0$ → **correct**, no update.

End of Iteration 2: $\mathbf{a} = (0, 0, 2)^T$ (no further change). The perceptron has converged in 2 iterations to $\mathbf{a} = (0,0,2)^T$, which classifies all four samples correctly. The decision boundary is the line $0 \cdot x_1 + 0 \cdot x_2 + 2 = 0$, i.e., a degenerate case where the "best" separator is the horizontal line through the data center (since the samples form a symmetric cross).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams**

- **Linear discriminant function** is the workhorse of statistical pattern recognition: $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$.
- The **decision boundary** $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ is a **hyperplane** in $d$-dimensional space.
- $\mathbf{w}$ is the **normal** to the hyperplane; $w_0$ shifts the hyperplane from the origin.
- **Case 1** ($\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$): discriminant reduces to $\mathbf{w}_i = \boldsymbol{\mu}_i / \sigma^2$.
- **Case 2** ($\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_i = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$): discriminant reduces to $\mathbf{w}_i = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_i$.
- The quadratic term $\mathbf{x}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ cancels across classes when covariances are equal — this is **why** the discriminant is linear.
- The hyperplane is **perpendicular to the line connecting the means** and (with equal priors) passes through the **midpoint**.
- **Augmented form** $\mathbf{y} = [\mathbf{x}; 1]$ and $\mathbf{a} = [\mathbf{w}; w_0]$ simplifies all derivations.
- **Perceptron criterion** $J_p = \sum (-\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y})$ only works for **separable** data.
- **Perceptron update rule** (on-line): $\mathbf{a}(k+1) = \mathbf{a}(k) + \eta(k)\mathbf{y}_k$ for misclassified $\mathbf{y}_k$.
- **MSE solution** $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{Y}^{\dagger}\mathbf{b}$ works for **any** data (separable or not) but minimizes squared error, not classification error.
- The **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{Y}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{Y}^T \mathbf{Y})^{-1}\mathbf{Y}^T$ is the key tool for the MSE procedure.
- **Multiclass extension**: use $c$ discriminants or a weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ of size $d \times c$.
- **Generalized linear discriminants** use $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ instead of $\mathbf{x}$, allowing nonlinear boundaries in the original space.
- **Bayes optimality:** Linear discriminants are **Bayes optimal** when features are Gaussian with equal covariance.
- **Convergence of perceptron** is guaranteed by the **Rosenblatt-Nilsson theorem** for separable data only.
- **Linear separability** is a geometric property, not a probabilistic one; small overlaps in distributions can prevent it.
- For KTU problems, always state the **form** of the discriminant (linear/quadratic) **before** substituting values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Final Tip:** When a numerical problem gives means and covariances, your **first move** must be to write down the discriminant function **template** with $\mathbf{w}_i$ and $w_{i0}$ in symbolic form, **then** plug in numbers. This earns you 1–2 marks for structure even if arithmetic fails later. The KTU 2024 scheme marks partial derivation heavily.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Component Analysis: Principal Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT503 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis: Principal Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Principal Component Analysis: Principal Components

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component (PC) Definition**
> A *Principal Component* of a random vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p)^T$ with covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is the linear combination
> $$Y_i = \mathbf{e}_i^{\,T} \mathbf{X} = e_{i1} X_1 + e_{i2} X_2 + \cdots + e_{ip} X_p$$
> where the coefficient vector $\mathbf{e}_i$ is the **eigenvector** of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ corresponding to the $i^{\text{th}}$ largest eigenvalue $\lambda_i$. The $i^{\text{th}}$ principal component $Y_i$ is thus a derived (latent) variable that captures the $i^{\text{th}}$ largest source of total variation in the data.

The full ordered set $\{Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_p\}$ constitutes the **Principal Component System** — an orthogonal rotation of the original coordinate axes so that the new axes (the principal components) are aligned with the directions of maximum variance in $\mathbb{R}^p$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing inside a **foggy cloud of data points** shaped like an elongated rugby ball (a 3-D ellipsoid) floating in space. The cloud is stretched more along one direction than the others. The "longest axis" of this rugby ball — the direction along which the points are most spread out — is the **First Principal Component (PC1)**. The next-longest axis, perpendicular to PC1, is the **Second Principal Component (PC2)**, and so on.

- If you had to **summarize** the cloud using just **one line**, you would choose the line of maximum spread (PC1).
- If you could use **two lines**, you would add the next-best perpendicular direction (PC2).
- The length of each axis of the ellipsoid is proportional to $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ (the square root of the corresponding eigenvalue).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PCA Works (Engineering Intuition):**
> In high-dimensional engineering datasets (e.g., 50 sensor readings from a turbine), variables are usually **correlated**. PCA exploits this redundancy: it compresses correlated information into a small number of uncorrelated components, dramatically reducing dimensionality while preserving maximum information (variance).

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Definitions)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mean Vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ | $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}] = (\mathbb{E}[X_1], \ldots, \mathbb{E}[X_p])^T$ |
| **Covariance Matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ | $\mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T]$ — symmetric, positive semi-definite $p \times p$ matrix |
| **Eigenvalues** $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_p \ge 0$ | Variances of successive PCs (always non-negative) |
| **Eigenvectors** $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_p$ | Loading vectors (directions) of the PCs; orthonormal: $\mathbf{e}_i^T \mathbf{e}_j = \delta_{ij}$ |
| **Scores** | The actual transformed coordinates: $y_{ij} = \mathbf{e}_i^T (\mathbf{x}_j - \boldsymbol{\mu})$ |

### 1.4 Assumptions Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Assumption Checklist (Always State These):**
> 1. The data cloud is **mean-centered** (or you explicitly center it): $\mathbf{x}_j \leftarrow \mathbf{x}_j - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$.
> 2. The covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ exists and is **finite**.
> 3. The variables are measured on **comparable scales**, or you have standardized them (correlation matrix PCA). KTU boards typically mark the choice between $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ (covariance) and $\mathbf{R}$ (correlation) explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric / Variational Principle

Principal components are derived from a single optimization principle:

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Optimization (Variance Maximization)**
> The $k^{\text{th}}$ principal component $Y_k = \mathbf{e}_k^T \mathbf{X}$ is the linear combination whose coefficients form a unit vector ($\mathbf{e}_k^T \mathbf{e}_k = 1$) that **maximizes** $\text{Var}(Y_k) = \mathbf{e}_k^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_k$ subject to the orthogonality constraint $\mathbf{e}_k^T \mathbf{e}_j = 0$ for all $j < k$.

## 2.2 Five Core Properties of Principal Components

For a $p$-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{X}$ with covariance $\mathbf{\Sigma}$:

### Property 1 — Variance of Each PC
$$\text{Var}(Y_i) = \mathbf{e}_i^{\,T} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_i = \lambda_i$$
($\lambda_i$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ eigenvalue of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$).

### Property 2 — Uncorrelatedness (Orthogonality of PCs)
$$\text{Cov}(Y_i, Y_j) = \mathbf{e}_i^{\,T} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_j = 0 \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$
This is the **single most important property** — the new coordinate system is statistically uncorrelated, even when the original variables are highly correlated.

### Property 3 — Total Variance Preservation
$$\sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{Var}(Y_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{Var}(X_i)$$
PCA does **not destroy variance**; it merely redistributes it across orthogonal axes.

### Property 4 — Proportion of Variance Explained (PVE)
$$\text{PVE}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_p}$$
The KTU board almost always tests the **cumulative PVE**:
$$\text{Cumulative PVE}_m = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}$$

### Property 5 — Reconstruction / Spectral Decomposition
The covariance matrix is decomposed as:
$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{E} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{E}^T$$
where $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{e}_1 \, \vert \, \mathbf{e}_2 \, \vert \, \cdots \, \vert \, \mathbf{e}_p]$ and $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_p)$.

## 2.3 Standardization vs. Covariance (Critical KTU Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choice of Input Matrix — KTU Board Favourite Question**
> - Use **Covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$** when variables are on the **same scale** (e.g., all lengths in meters).
> - Use **Correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$** when variables are on **different scales** (e.g., height in cm and weight in kg). $\mathbf{R}$ is simply $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ applied to the standardized variables $Z_j = (X_j - \mu_j)/\sigma_j$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Interpretation / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$ (centered) | Sample covariance matrix (where $\mathbf{X}_c$ is $n \times p$ mean-centered) |
| 2 | $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{e}_i$ | Eigen equation to solve for PCs |
| 3 | $Y_i = \mathbf{e}_i^{\,T} \mathbf{X}$ | The $i^{\text{th}}$ principal component (random variable form) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{E}^T \mathbf{X}$ | Full PCA transformation matrix equation |
| 5 | $\text{Var}(Y_i) = \lambda_i$ | Variance of the $i^{\text{th}}$ PC |
| 6 | $\text{Cov}(Y_i, Y_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | PCs are uncorrelated |
| 7 | $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma})$ | Total variance = trace |
| 8 | $\prod_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i = \vert \mathbf{\Sigma} \vert$ | Total variance product = determinant (generalized variance) |
| 9 | $\text{PVE}_k = \lambda_k / \sum \lambda_i$ | Proportion of variance explained by $k^{\text{th}}$ PC |
| 10 | $\text{Cumulative PVE}_m = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, / \, \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i$ | Information retained by first $m$ PCs (criterion: $\ge 0.80$ or $0.90$) |
| 11 | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{E} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{E}^T$ | Spectral decomposition of covariance |
| 12 | $\mathbf{R}_{ij} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$ | Correlation coefficient (input when variables differ in scale) |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Field | Application of Principal Components |
|---|---|
| **Image Processing** | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), compression, denoising |
| **Biomedical Signals** | EEG/ECG feature extraction, removing ocular artifacts |
| **Finance** | Risk-factor decomposition, portfolio variance analysis |
| **Quality Control** | Multivariate SPC charts using $T^2 = \sum y_i^2 / \lambda_i$ |
| **Machine Learning** | Pre-processing step to combat the **curse of dimensionality** |
| **Mechanical Vibration** | Modal analysis — extracting dominant vibration modes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation of Eigenvalues in Engineering:**
> In vibration analysis, the **square roots of eigenvalues** $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ are the **semi-axis lengths** of the data ellipsoid. A large gap between $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ (scree plot "elbow") indicates the data is essentially **one-dimensional** — a strong engineering simplification.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First Principal Component via Lagrange Multipliers

**Goal:** Find the unit vector $\mathbf{e}_1$ that maximizes the variance of the linear combination $Y_1 = \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{X}$.

**Step 1 — Setup the objective function and constraint.**

We wish to maximize
$$\text{Var}(Y_1) = \text{Var}(\mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}$$
subject to the unit-length constraint
$$\mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Formulate the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{e}, \lambda) = \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e} - \lambda \left( \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e} - 1 \right)$$
Here $\lambda$ is the Lagrange multiplier.

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{e}$ and set to zero.**

Using the matrix differentiation rule $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{e}} \left( \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{e} \right) = 2 \mathbf{A} \mathbf{e}$ (for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{e}} = 2 \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to the eigenvalue problem.**

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e} = \lambda \mathbf{e}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the optimal solution.**

This is precisely the **eigenvalue equation** for $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. Therefore the optimum direction $\mathbf{e}_1$ must be an eigenvector of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. To maximize variance, we choose the eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$:

$$\mathbf{e}_1 = \text{eigenvector of } \mathbf{\Sigma} \text{ corresponding to } \lambda_1 = \lambda_{\max}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the maximum variance.**

Pre-multiplying the eigenvalue equation by $\mathbf{e}_1^T$:
$$\mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_1 = \lambda_1 \mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_1 = \lambda_1 \cdot 1 = \lambda_1$$

Therefore $\text{Var}(Y_1) = \lambda_1$, the largest eigenvalue. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second Principal Component (Uncorrelatedness)

**Goal:** Find $\mathbf{e}_2$ that maximizes $\mathbf{e}_2^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_2$ subject to **two** constraints:
$$\mathbf{e}_2^T \mathbf{e}_2 = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_2 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Lagrangian with two multipliers.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{e}_2, \lambda, \alpha) = \mathbf{e}_2^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_2 - \lambda \left( \mathbf{e}_2^T \mathbf{e}_2 - 1 \right) - \alpha \, \mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{e}_2$.**

$$2 \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_2 - 2 \lambda \mathbf{e}_2 - \alpha \mathbf{e}_1 = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 — Pre-multiply by $\mathbf{e}_1^T$ and use $\mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{\Sigma} = \lambda_1 \mathbf{e}_1^T$.**

$$2 \lambda_1 \mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_2 - 2 \lambda \mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_2 - \alpha \mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_1 = 0$$

Since $\mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_2 = 0$ and $\mathbf{e}_1^T \mathbf{e}_1 = 1$:
$$- \alpha = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\alpha = 0$ back.**

$$2 \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_2 - 2 \lambda \mathbf{e}_2 = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{e}_2 = \lambda \mathbf{e}_2$$

**Step 5 — Choose the second-largest eigenvalue.**

The vector $\mathbf{e}_2$ is therefore also an eigenvector of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. To satisfy the orthogonality constraint, we choose the eigenvector corresponding to the **second largest** eigenvalue $\lambda_2$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursive Result:** By induction, $\mathbf{e}_k$ is the eigenvector of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ corresponding to the $k^{\text{th}}$ largest eigenvalue $\lambda_k$, and the $k^{\text{th}}$ principal component $Y_k$ has variance $\lambda_k$. All pairs of distinct PCs are uncorrelated.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Variance Equality

Starting from the spectral decomposition:
$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i \mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^T$$

Take the trace (sum of diagonal elements):

$$\text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{Var}(X_i) = \text{tr}\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i \mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^T \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i \, \text{tr}(\mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^T)$$

Since $\text{tr}(\mathbf{e}_i \mathbf{e}_i^T) = \mathbf{e}_i^T \mathbf{e}_i = 1$:

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{Var}(X_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \text{Var}(Y_i)}$$

This proves that **PCA redistributes but does not change the total variance**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (2-Variable Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Numerical Standard:** 2-variable covariance matrix with hand-calculable eigenvalues.

**Problem:** Let $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Find the principal components and the proportion of variance explained.

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation.**

$$\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$$
$$(4 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - 1 \cdot 1 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 8 - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 7 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$\lambda = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 28}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{8}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm 2\sqrt{2}}{2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{2}$$

So $\lambda_1 = 3 + \sqrt{2} \approx 4.4142$ and $\lambda_2 = 3 - \sqrt{2} \approx 1.5858$.

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$.**

$$(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I}) \mathbf{e}_1 = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda_1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 - \lambda_1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} e_{11} \\ e_{12} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

With $\lambda_1 = 3 + \sqrt{2}$:
$$4 - (3 + \sqrt{2}) = 1 - \sqrt{2}, \quad 2 - (3 + \sqrt{2}) = -1 - \sqrt{2}$$
Row 1: $(1 - \sqrt{2}) e_{11} + e_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow e_{12} = (\sqrt{2} - 1) e_{11}$

Using $e_{11}^2 + e_{12}^2 = 1$:
$$e_{11}^2 \left[ 1 + (\sqrt{2} - 1)^2 \right] = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{11}^2 \left[ 1 + 2 - 2\sqrt{2} + 1 \right] = 1$$
$$e_{11}^2 (4 - 2\sqrt{2}) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{11} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 - 2\sqrt{2}}}$$

After rationalization, $e_{11} \approx 0.9239$, $e_{12} \approx 0.3827$.

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2$ (orthogonal to $\mathbf{e}_1$).**

$$\mathbf{e}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.3827 \\ 0.9239 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Principal components.**

$$Y_1 = 0.9239 \, X_1 + 0.3827 \, X_2, \quad \text{Var}(Y_1) = 4.4142$$
$$Y_2 = -0.3827 \, X_1 + 0.9239 \, X_2, \quad \text{Var}(Y_2) = 1.5858$$

**Step 6 — PVE calculation.**

Total variance $= \text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}) = 4 + 2 = 6 = 4.4142 + 1.5858$ ✓ (confirms Property 3)

$$\text{PVE}_1 = \frac{4.4142}{6} \times 100\% \approx 73.57\%$$
$$\text{Cumulative PVE}_2 = \frac{4.4142 + 1.5858}{6} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Conclusion:** PC1 alone captures **~73.6%** of total variance, justifying a one-dimensional summary if dimensionality reduction is required.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Computational Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Raw Data Matrix X of size n by p"] --> B["Step 1: Compute Mean Vector mu of X"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Mean Center the Data: Xc = X minus mu"]
    C --> D{"Step 3: Choose Input Matrix Type"}
    D -- "Same Scale Variables" --> E["Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma = Xc transpose Xc by n minus 1"]
    D -- "Different Scales" --> F["Standardize to Z scores and Compute Correlation Matrix R"]
    E --> G["Step 4: Solve Eigen Equation: Sigma e = lambda e"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Step 5: Sort Eigenvalues in Descending Order: lambda1 greater than lambda2 ... greater than lambdap"]
    H --> I["Step 6: Select Top k Eigenvectors forming Matrix E sub k of size p by k"]
    I --> J["Step 7: Project Data: Y = E sub k transpose times Xc"]
    J --> K["Step 8: Validate via Scree Plot and Cumulative PVE greater than or equal to 0.80"]
    K --> L["END: Principal Component Scores Y of size n by k"]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation of Principal Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Axes X1 horizontal and X2 vertical"] --> B["Data Ellipse tilted in original frame"]
    B --> C["PCA Rotation by angle theta equal to arctan of e12 over e11"]
    C --> D["New Axes Y1 along major axis and Y2 along minor axis"]
    D --> E["Variance of Y1 equals lambda1 is largest"]
    D --> F["Variance of Y2 equals lambda2 is smallest"]
    D --> G["Covariance of Y1 and Y2 equals 0 fully uncorrelated"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (PC Extraction Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        I1["n observations of p variables"]
        I2["Raw data matrix X with n rows p columns"]
    end

    subgraph PREPROC["PREPROCESSING BLOCK"]
        P1["Column-wise mean subtraction"]
        P2["Optional scaling by standard deviation"]
        P3["Centered matrix Xc"]
    end

    subgraph COVARIANCE["COVARIANCE BLOCK"]
        C1["Compute p by p Sigma or R"]
        C2["Symmetric positive semi-definite matrix"]
    end

    subgraph EIGEN["EIGEN-DECOMPOSITION BLOCK"]
        E1["Solve characteristic equation"]
        E2["Sort eigenvalues lambda1 to lambda p descending"]
        E3["Form orthonormal eigenvector matrix E"]
    end

    subgraph SELECTION["SELECTION BLOCK"]
        S1["Scree plot inspection"]
        S2["Kaiser rule lambda greater than 1 for R matrix"]
        S3["Cumulative PVE threshold greater than or equal to 0.80 or 0.90"]
        S4["Choose k less than p"]
    end

    subgraph TRANSFORM["TRANSFORMATION BLOCK"]
        T1["Reduced eigenvector matrix E sub k"]
        T2["Compute scores Y = E sub k transpose Xc"]
        T3["Output n by k score matrix"]
    end

    I1 --> I2 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> C1 --> C2 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3
```

## 4.4 Variance Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Variance in Original Space: sum of Var Xi equals tr Sigma"] --> B["Variance of Principal Component 1: lambda1 LARGEST"]
    A --> C["Variance of Principal Component 2: lambda2"]
    A --> D["Variance of Principal Component 3: lambda3"]
    A --> E["Variance of Principal Component p: lambda p SMALLEST"]
    B --> F["Cumulative Variance lambda1 by sum"]
    C --> G["Cumulative Variance lambda1 plus lambda2 by sum"]
    D --> H["Cumulative Variance lambda1 plus lambda2 plus lambda3 by sum"]
    E --> I["Final Cumulative Variance equals 1 or 100 percent"]
```
