```mermaid
    L2 --> M
    M --> N[Reproduce Correlation Matrix R hat L L prime plus Psi]
    N --> O[Residual Matrix R minus R hat]
    O --> P[Rotation and Interpretation]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Schematic — What Each Algorithm Optimizes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        R[Correlation Matrix R]
    end
    R --> PCM[Principal Component Method]
    R --> PAF[Principal Axis Factoring]
    R --> MLF[Maximum Likelihood]
    PCM --> O1[Objective Maximize sum of top m eigenvalues]
    PAF --> O2[Objective Reproduce R minus Psi using m factors]
    MLF --> O3[Objective Maximize likelihood under MVN assumption]
    O1 --> C1[Communalities equal 1]
    O2 --> C2[Communalities less than 1]
    O3 --> C3[Enables significance testing]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Extraction Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is data approximately multivariate normal]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Need statistical significance tests]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use PAF or PCM]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Choose Maximum Likelihood]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Choose Principal Axis Factoring]
    Q3 --> Q4[Is your goal data reduction]
    Q4 -- Yes --> R3[Choose Principal Component Method]
    Q4 -- No --> R2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam mnemonic:** *P*CM reduces **P**redictors; **P**AF finds **P**ure latent constructs; **M**L fits a **M**odel statistically. If the question says "without statistical testing," eliminate ML.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 1]`
**Differentiate between Principal Component Method and Principal Axis Factoring for factor extraction.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Principal Component Method (PCM) | Principal Axis Factoring (PAF) |
|---|---|---|
| Input matrix | $\mathbf{R}$ directly | Reduced matrix $\mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{R} - \mathbf{\hat{\Psi}}$ |
| Treatment of $\mathbf{\Psi}$ | Ignored (implicitly absorbed into discarded eigenvalues) | Explicitly estimated and subtracted |
| Communalities | Forced to equal 1 | Strictly less than 1 |
| Objective | Maximize variance explained by first $m$ PCs | Reproduce common variance using $m$ common factors |
| Iteration | Non-iterative | Iterative until convergence |

Hence PCM is mathematically simpler but conceptually a *data-reduction* tool, while PAF is the **true factor-analytic** extraction.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2]`
**State the Maximum Likelihood discrepancy function used for factor extraction. Mention the role of the Bartlett correction.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The ML discrepancy function minimized during extraction is

$$
F_{ML}(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{\Psi}) = \text{tr}\bigl[(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{R} - \mathbf{I})^{2}\bigr] - \log\vert\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{R}\vert + (p - m)
$$

The **Bartlett correction** scales $F_{ML}$ to obtain a chi-square statistic that tests whether $m$ factors are sufficient:

$$
\chi^{2}_{ML} = \bigl[n - 1 - (2p + 5)/6 - 2m/3\bigr]\;F_{ML} \sim \chi^{2}_{\nu}, \quad \nu = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(p-m)^{2} - (p+m)\bigr]
$$

It improves finite-sample approximation to the chi-square distribution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Sessional Model 1]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **iterative algorithm** for Principal Axis Factoring. List the conditions for convergence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the $4 \times 4$ correlation matrix

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix}
1.00 & 0.60 & 0.50 & 0.40 \\
0.60 & 1.00 & 0.55 & 0.45 \\
0.50 & 0.55 & 1.00 & 0.50 \\
0.40 & 0.45 & 0.50 & 1.00
\end{bmatrix}
$$

compute the **initial communality estimates** using the squared multiple correlation method and form the **reduced correlation matrix** $\mathbf{R}^{*}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Iterative PAF Algorithm — 7 marks**

1. **Step 1 (Initialization):** Compute initial communality estimates $h_i^{2\,(0)}$ for each variable $i = 1,\dots,p$. Common choices:
   - SMC: $h_i^{2\,(0)} = 1 - 1/r^{ii}$
   - Max-absolute-row: $h_i^{2\,(0)} = \max_{j \ne i}\vert r_{ij}\vert$
2. **Step 2 (Reduced matrix):** Form $\mathbf{R}^{*(0)} = \mathbf{R} - \text{diag}(1 - h_1^{2\,(0)}, \dots, 1 - h_p^{2\,(0)})$.
3. **Step 3 (Eigendecomposition):** Compute eigenpairs $(\lambda_k^{*(0)}, \mathbf{e}_k^{*(0)})$ of $\mathbf{R}^{*(0)}$ and order eigenvalues descending.
4. **Step 4 (Loadings):** Retain the $m$ positive eigenpairs and compute $\boldsymbol{\ell}_k^{(0)} = \sqrt{\lambda_k^{*(0)}}\,\mathbf{e}_k^{*(0)}$.
5. **Step 5 (Update communalities):** $h_i^{2\,(1)} = \sum_{k=1}^{m}[\ell_{ik}^{(0)}]^2$.
6. **Step 6 (Convergence check):** If $\max_i \vert h_i^{2\,(t+1)} - h_i^{2\,(t)}\vert < \epsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$ or $10^{-6}$), stop; else set $t \leftarrow t+1$ and go to Step 2.

**Convergence conditions:** (i) $\mathbf{R}^{*(t)}$ remains positive semi-definite, (ii) at least $m$ eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}^{*(t)}$ stay positive, (iii) communality increments shrink monotonically, (iv) maximum iteration limit (e.g., 100) not exceeded.

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

First compute $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$. The determinant of $\mathbf{R}$ is obtained by cofactor expansion (or by direct software):

$$
\det(\mathbf{R}) = 0.2991
$$

Computing the adjugate matrix gives the diagonal entries of $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ as:

$$
r^{11} = 1.965, \quad r^{22} = 1.873, \quad r^{33} = 1.799, \quad r^{44} = 1.681
$$

Initial SMC-based communalities:

$$
h_1^{2\,(0)} = 1 - \tfrac{1}{1.965} = 0.4911
$$
$$
h_2^{2\,(0)} = 1 - \tfrac{1}{1.873} = 0.4661
$$
$$
h_3^{2\,(0)} = 1 - \tfrac{1}{1.799} = 0.4441
$$
$$
h_4^{2\,(0)} = 1 - \tfrac{1}{1.681} = 0.4051
$$

Specific variances: $\psi_1^{(0)} = 0.5089$, $\psi_2^{(0)} = 0.5339$, $\psi_3^{(0)} = 0.5559$, $\psi_4^{(0)} = 0.5949$.

Reduced matrix:

$$
\mathbf{R}^{*(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.4911 & 0.60 & 0.50 & 0.40 \\
0.60 & 0.4661 & 0.55 & 0.45 \\
0.50 & 0.55 & 0.4441 & 0.50 \\
0.40 & 0.45 & 0.50 & 0.4051
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The off-diagonal entries are unchanged; only the diagonal has been replaced by the SMC communality estimates. This $\mathbf{R}^{*(0)}$ is now ready for eigendecomposition and factor extraction.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Sessional Model 2]` (14 Marks) **(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**(a)** Describe the **Maximum Likelihood method** for factor extraction. What assumptions are required? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Spectral Decomposition Theorem** as applied to the Principal Component Method. Show that under PCM, the reproduced correlation matrix $\mathbf{\hat{R}} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\prime}$ reproduces the diagonal exactly. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Maximum Likelihood Method — 7 marks**

**Assumptions:**
- $\mathbf{X}_1, \dots, \mathbf{X}_n \overset{iid}{\sim} N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{\Sigma})$ (multivariate normality).
- Orthogonal common factor model: $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\prime} + \mathbf{\Psi}$ with $\mathbf{\Psi}$ diagonal and positive definite.
- Common factors $\mathbf{F}$ and specific factors $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ are independent with $\mathbf{F} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_m)$, $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Psi})$.

**Algorithm:** The log-likelihood (up to constants) is

$$
\ell(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{\Psi}) = -\frac{n}{2}\bigl[\log\vert\mathbf{\Sigma}\vert + \text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{S})\bigr]
$$

ML estimators $(\hat{\mathbf{L}}_{ML}, \hat{\mathbf{\Psi}}_{ML})$ are obtained by **maximizing** $\ell$ subject to the structural constraint $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{\prime} + \mathbf{\Psi}$. This is typically done by:

1. **E-step:** Given current $\mathbf{\Sigma}^{(t)}$, compute expected sufficient statistics $E[\mathbf{F}\vert\mathbf{X}]$ and $E[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^{\prime}\vert\mathbf{X}]$.
2. **M-step:** Maximize expected complete-data log-likelihood to obtain $\mathbf{\Sigma}^{(t+1)}$.
3. Iterate until $\vert F_{ML}^{(t+1)} - F_{ML}^{(t)}\vert < \epsilon$.

The **Bartlett-corrected chi-square statistic** then enables formal hypothesis testing of $m$-factor sufficiency.

**(b) Spectral Decomposition and PCM Diagonal Reproduction — 7 marks**

The **Spectral Decomposition Theorem** states that any real symmetric matrix $\mathbf{R}$ admits the orthogonal decomposition

$$
\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{E} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{E}^{\prime}
$$

where $\mathbf{E}$ is orthogonal (columns are eigenvectors) and $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_p)$ with $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_p \ge 0$.

Under PCM we retain the **first $m$ eigenpairs** and define the loading matrix $\mathbf{L}_{PC} = \mathbf{E}_m \mathbf{\Lambda}_m^{1/2}$, where $\mathbf{E}_m = [\mathbf{e}_1 \cdots \mathbf{e}_m]$ and $\mathbf{\Lambda}_m = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_m)$.

Then

$$
\mathbf{L}_{PC} \mathbf{L}_{PC}^{\prime} = \mathbf{E}_m \mathbf{\Lambda}_m^{1/2} \mathbf{\Lambda}_m^{1/2} \mathbf{E}_m^{\prime} = \mathbf{E}_m \mathbf{\Lambda}_m \mathbf{E}_m^{\prime} = \mathbf{R}_m
$$

The diagonal entries of $\mathbf{R}_m$ are

$$
[\mathbf{R}_m]_{ii} = \sum_{k=1}^{m}\lambda_k e_{ik}^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{m}\ell_{ik}^{2} = h_i^{2\,(PCM)}
$$

Since $\mathbf{R}_m$ differs from $\mathbf{R}$ only by the discarded $(p-m)$ eigenvalue terms, its diagonal sums to

$$
\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_m) = \sum_{k=1}^{m}\lambda_k \le \text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = p
$$

with equality **iff** $m = p$. Thus the diagonal of the PCM-reproduced correlation matrix is **strictly less than 1** when $m < p$ and equals 1 only when no factor is discarded — making PCM a strict *approximation* method, not an exact factor model.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in extraction questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PCM with PAF:** Stating that PCM involves specific variances $\psi_i$ — it does *not*. PCM communalities are forced to 1.
> 2. **Forgetting the iteration:** Writing "form $\mathbf{R}^{*}$ and eigen-decompose" without mentioning the convergence loop in PAF.
> 3. **Misapplying $\sqrt{\lambda_k}$:** Writing $\ell_k = \lambda_k \mathbf{e}_k$ instead of $\sqrt{\lambda_k}\,\mathbf{e}_k$. The square root is **non-negotiable**.
> 4. **Ignoring sign indeterminacy:** Not mentioning that $-\sqrt{\lambda_k}\,\mathbf{e}_k$ is equally valid; loadings are identified only up to sign.
> 5. **Skipping the communality update step:** Forgetting that $h_i^{2\,(t+1)} = \sum_k \ell_{ik}^{2\,(t)}$ must be computed before the next iteration's reduced matrix is formed.
> 6. **Bartlett test formula:** Writing $\nu = p - m$ instead of the correct $\nu = \frac{1}{2}[(p-m)^2 - (p+m)]$.
> 7. **Standardizing the data:** Forgetting that $\mathbf{R}$ (correlation matrix) requires *standardized* variables — otherwise eigen-decompose $\mathbf{S}$, not $\mathbf{R}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Extracting Common Factors":**

- **Definition:** Extraction = estimating $(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{\Psi})$ from $\mathbf{R}$ using a pre-chosen $m$.
- **PCM** eigen-decomposes $\mathbf{R}$ directly: $\boldsymbol{\ell}_k = \sqrt{\lambda_k}\,\mathbf{e}_k$; communalities = 1; non-iterative.
- **PAF** uses $\mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{R} - \hat{\mathbf{\Psi}}$, iterates until $\max_i \vert \Delta h_i^2 \vert < \epsilon$; requires positive eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}^{*}$.
- **ML** maximizes normal-theory likelihood; requires iterative EM/Newton–Raphson; permits chi-square testing via Bartlett's correction.
- **Reduced matrix** $\mathbf{R}^{*}$ has off-diagonal entries equal to $\mathbf{R}$ and diagonal entries equal to current $h_i^{2\,(t)}$.
- **Loading formula:** ALWAYS $\sqrt{\lambda_k}$, never $\lambda_k$.
- **Convergence criterion:** $\max_i \vert h_i^{2\,(t+1)} - h_i^{2\,(t)}\vert < 10^{-4}$.
- **Communalities** $\in [0,1]$; **specific variances** $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$; sum of communalities equals total common variance.
- **Variance explained by factor $k$:** $\lambda_k^{*}/\sum_j \lambda_j^{*}$ (or $\lambda_k/p$ for PCM).
- **Bartlett test degrees of freedom:** $\nu = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(p-m)^2 - (p+m)\bigr]$.
- **Sign indeterminacy:** $(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{T})(\mathbf{L}, \mathbf{T})^{\prime} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}\mathbf{T}^{\prime}\mathbf{L}^{\prime}$ for any orthogonal $\mathbf{T}$ — hence rotation comes *after* extraction.
- **Practical tip for KTU exams:** If asked to "extract factors by PAF" on a small matrix, present the *first iteration in full* and state "iteration proceeds until convergence" — full multi-iteration tables are rarely required.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determining Number of Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT503 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Determining Number of Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Factor Analysis** is a multivariate statistical technique whose primary objective is to represent a set of $p$ observed variables in terms of a smaller number $m$ of unobserved latent variables called **common factors**. The process of **Determining the Number of Factors** is the crucial pre-extraction decision step in Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) that specifies how many latent constructs adequately explain the covariance structure of the original variable set.

Mathematically, the standard orthogonal factor model is expressed as:

$$X = \mu + LF + \epsilon$$

Where $X$ is the $p \times 1$ vector of observed variables, $\mu$ is the $p \times 1$ mean vector, $L$ is the $p \times m$ matrix of **factor loadings**, $F$ is the $m \times 1$ vector of **common factors**, and $\epsilon$ is the $p \times 1$ vector of **specific (unique) factors** (errors).

The covariance structure implied by the model is:

$$\Sigma = LL^T + \Psi$$

Where $LL^T$ is the **common variance** matrix and $\Psi = \text{diag}(\psi_1, \psi_2, \ldots, \psi_p)$ is the diagonal **specific variance** matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The number of factors $m$ is determined by analyzing the eigenvalues of either the correlation matrix $R$ or the reduced correlation matrix $R - \Psi$. The factor model is identified only when $m$ is fixed *before* the loading matrix $L$ is rotated and estimated.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The School Bag Problem" 🎒**
>
> Imagine 10 students carry a school bag full of 30 different items (textbooks, stationery, snacks, sports gear, art supplies, etc.). You notice that students who carry geometry boxes also tend to carry mathematical instruments, while students with sketch pens also carry paint. There are hidden *patterns* in what they carry.
>
> Instead of listing all 30 items, a smart observer realizes: *"Behind these 30 items, there are really only 3 hidden 'themes' — Academic kit, Sports kit, and Art kit."*
>
> **Determining the number of factors is exactly this:** finding how many hidden "themes" (factors) are needed to explain the correlations among the 30 items (variables). Carry too many themes and you overcomplicate the bag. Carry too few and items get misfiled into the wrong theme.

The **physical/engineering utility** lies in **dimensionality reduction**. In engineering, we frequently encounter datasets with hundreds of correlated sensor readings (e.g., vibration sensors, temperature sensors, strain gauges). Determining the correct number of latent factors helps in:

- **Signal Processing:** Separating mixed signals into independent sources.
- **Structural Engineering:** Identifying primary modes of structural vibration.
- **Machine Learning Preprocessing:** Reducing feature redundancy.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Factor Retention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics in Bold:**
> - **Eigenvalue ($\lambda$):** The amount of variance in the original variables explained by a single factor.
> - **Total Variance:** The sum of all eigenvalues equals $p$ (the number of variables) when working on a correlation matrix.
> - **Proportion of Variance Explained (PVE):** $\text{PVE}_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i} = \frac{\lambda_j}{p}$
> - **Cumulative Variance:** $C_m = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_j}{p}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Principal Methods for Determining $m$

The number of factors $m$ is a **parameter choice** problem, and the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following six methods. Each addresses the trade-off between **parsimony** (few factors) and **fit** (capturing the correlation structure).

---

#### Method 1: Kaiser’s Criterion (Eigenvalue > 1 Rule)

This is the simplest, most widely taught method. It is based on a single principle: *a factor should explain at least as much variance as a single standardized variable.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** A standardized variable has variance 1. If a factor's eigenvalue is less than 1, it explains less variance than one original variable, so it is deemed **useless**.

**Procedure:**
1. Compute the correlation matrix $R$ of the $p$ variables.
2. Extract the eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_p$.
3. Retain all factors with $\lambda_j > 1$.

**Advantages:** Objective, fast, easy to apply.
**Disadvantages:** Tends to **over-extract** factors (especially with $p > 30$ or non-normal data). Arbitrary threshold of 1.

---

#### Method 2: Scree Plot (Cattell’s Scree Test)

A **graphical** method introduced by Raymond Cattell (1966). The eigenvalues are plotted on the y-axis and the factor number on the x-axis, forming a downward curve. The number of factors retained is the number of eigenvalues *before* the curve flattens into a "scree" (debris/rubble) line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cattell’s Logic:** The steep portion of the curve represents the "true" factors. The shallow tail (the *scree*) represents noise that should be discarded.

**Disadvantages:** Highly subjective. Two analysts may retain different numbers. Works better with $p \geq 20$.

---

#### Method 3: Cumulative Variance Criterion

Retain the minimum number of factors such that the **cumulative proportion of variance explained** reaches a pre-specified threshold (commonly **70%**, **75%**, or **80%**).

$$m = \min\left\{k : \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_j}{p} \geq T\right\}$$

Where $T$ is the threshold, typically $T = 0.70$ to $0.80$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** In social science research, **70%** is the standard. In physical/engineering sciences where variance structure is often tighter, **80%** is preferred.

---

#### Method 4: Horn’s Parallel Analysis (Recommended Gold Standard)

Developed by John L. Horn (1965), this is a **Monte Carlo simulation-based** method that corrects Kaiser’s over-extraction bias.

**Procedure:**
1. Compute the eigenvalues $\lambda_j^{(real)}$ of the real correlation matrix.
2. Generate $B$ (typically $B = 1000$) random datasets of the same size $n \times p$ by **shuffling columns independently** to break all correlations.
3. Compute the **mean (or 95th percentile) of the random eigenvalues** $\bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)}$.
4. Retain factor $j$ if and only if: $\lambda_j^{(real)} > \bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Horn’s test is superior:** Random data will produce eigenvalues > 1 purely by sampling chance. Parallel analysis compares your *real* eigenvalues to the **chance baseline**, ensuring only meaningful factors are retained.

---

#### Method 5: Minimum Average Partial (MAP) Test — Velicer (1976)

This is a **principled, non-graphical** method that progressively partials out common variance and looks for the minimum residual.

**Procedure:**
1. Start with the original correlation matrix $R_0 = R$.
2. For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, p - 1$:
   - Partial out the influence of $k$ components from the correlation matrix.
   - Compute the average squared off-diagonal partial correlation: $f_k = \frac{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^{(k)2}}{p(p-1)}$.
3. The optimal number of factors is the value of $k$ at which $f_k$ is **minimized**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** $f_k$ measures the *remaining shared variance* after $k$ factors have been removed. When the minimum is reached, additional factors are only modeling noise.

---

#### Method 6: Information Criteria (BIC / AIC for Factor Models)

Information criteria penalize model complexity, balancing fit against parsimony. For factor models with maximum likelihood estimation:

$$BIC(m) = -2 \log L_m + q(m) \log(n)$$

$$AIC(m) = -2 \log L_m + 2q(m)$$

Where $q(m) = m(2p - m + 1)/2$ is the number of free parameters in a factor model with $m$ factors, $L_m$ is the maximized likelihood, and $n$ is the sample size. The optimal $m$ minimizes the criterion.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Core Formula | Decision Rule | Threshold (Bold) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kaiser’s Criterion | $\text{PVE}_j = \lambda_j / p$ | Retain if $\lambda_j > 1$ | **1.00** |
| 2 | Scree Plot | Visual inspection of elbow | Number of factors before the scree | Subjective |
| 3 | Cumulative Variance | $C_m = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j / p$ | First $m$ where $C_m \geq T$ | **T = 0.70 to 0.80** |
| 4 | Horn’s Parallel Analysis | $\lambda_j^{(real)}$ vs $\bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)}$ | Retain if real eigenvalue > random mean | **1000 permutations** |
| 5 | Velicer’s MAP | $f_k = \frac{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^{(k)2}}{p(p-1)}$ | Minimize $f_k$ over $k$ | $k = \arg\min f_k$ |
| 6 | Information Criteria | $BIC(m) = -2\ln L_m + q(m)\ln n$ | Minimize $BIC(m)$ or $AIC(m)$ | **$q(m) = m(2p - m + 1)/2$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Note:** Eigenvalues are dimensionless (variance units). Cumulative variance is a proportion in $[0, 1]$. $q(m)$ is a pure count of parameters.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Factor Number Determination |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Vibration Analysis** | Determining the number of dominant modal frequencies from accelerometer data. |
| **Image Processing (Face Recognition)** | Selecting how many eigenfaces to retain in PCA-based recognition systems. |
| **Financial Engineering** | Identifying the latent risk factors (market, sector, style) in asset returns. |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Determining independent components in EEG/MEG recordings. |
| **Structural Health Monitoring** | Separating environmental effects (temperature, humidity) from damage indicators. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Why the Eigenvalue Threshold of 1 in Kaiser’s Criterion

**Setup:** Consider the $p \times p$ correlation matrix $R$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_p$ and corresponding orthonormal eigenvectors $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_p$.

**Step 1 — Spectral Decomposition:** The correlation matrix can be decomposed as:

$$R = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j v_j v_j^T$$

**Step 2 — Total Variance Identity:** The total variance in standardized data is the trace of $R$:

$$\text{tr}(R) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \text{Var}(X_j) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} 1 = p$$

**Step 3 — Trace Equals Sum of Eigenvalues:** From the spectral decomposition, the trace of $R$ is also the sum of its eigenvalues:

$$\text{tr}(R) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j = p$$

**Step 4 — Variance Explained per Factor:** Each eigenvalue $\lambda_j$ represents the variance of the $j$-th principal component (and equivalently, the $j$-th factor in maximum likelihood EFA). The proportion of total variance captured by factor $j$ is:

$$\text{PVE}_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{p}$$

**Step 5 — Threshold Justification:** A single observed standardized variable has variance exactly 1. If factor $j$ has $\lambda_j < 1$, it captures less variance than the original variable itself. Therefore, the factor contributes no parsimony gain and should be discarded. The threshold becomes:

$$\lambda_j > 1 \iff \text{PVE}_j > \frac{1}{p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Retain all factors with $\lambda_j > 1$. For $p$ variables, the maximum number of useful factors is theoretically bounded by $p$, but in practice $m \ll p$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cumulative Variance Stopping Rule

**Step 1 — Variance Partition:** For a chosen number of factors $m$, the total variance explained is:

$$V_m = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j$$

**Step 2 — Proportion Explained:** The cumulative proportion is:

$$C_m = \frac{V_m}{p} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j}{p}$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule:** The optimal $m$ is the smallest integer satisfying:

$$C_m \geq T$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:** Suppose $p = 5$ and eigenvalues are $\lambda = (2.8, 1.4, 0.7, 0.05, 0.05)$. We require $T = 0.70$.

$$C_1 = \frac{2.8}{5} = 0.56 \quad (\text{below threshold})$$

$$C_2 = \frac{2.8 + 1.4}{5} = \frac{4.2}{5} = 0.84 \quad (\text{meets threshold!})$$

Therefore, **$m = 2$ factors** are retained.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Velicer’s MAP Test — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Initial Residual Matrix:** Let $R^{(0)} = R$ be the starting correlation matrix. The average squared off-diagonal correlation is:

$$f_0 = \frac{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^{(0)2}}{p(p-1)}$$

**Step 2 — First Partial Out (k = 1):** Partial out the influence of the first principal component by computing partial correlations. The partial correlation between $X_i$ and $X_j$ controlling for the first component $Z_1$ is:

$$r_{ij \cdot 1} = \frac{r_{ij} - r_{i1} r_{j1}}{\sqrt{(1 - r_{i1}^2)(1 - r_{j1}^2)}}$$

The new squared partial correlations are squared and averaged:

$$f_1 = \frac{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij \cdot 1}^{2}}{p(p-1)}$$

**Step 3 — General Iteration:** At step $k$, partial out $k$ components and compute $f_k$.

**Step 4 — Optimal Stopping:** The number of factors is:

$$m = \arg\min_{k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, p-1\}} f_k$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:** Suppose $p = 4$ and we obtain:

$$f_0 = 0.32, \quad f_1 = 0.18, \quad f_2 = 0.09, \quad f_3 = 0.11$$

Since the minimum occurs at $k = 2$, the optimal number of factors is **$m = 2$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is a stopping rule:** If we extract more factors than $m$, $f_k$ typically *increases* (the additional factors are modeling noise that adds no structure). The minimum of $f_k$ is the natural stopping point.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Horn’s Parallel Analysis Decision Statistic

**Step 1 — Random Eigenvalue Baseline:** For each factor position $j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$, compute the mean of random eigenvalues across $B$ permutations:

$$\bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \lambda_j^{(b, rand)}$$

**Step 2 — Adjusted Eigenvalues:** Horn proposed using adjusted eigenvalues:

$$\lambda_j^{adj} = \lambda_j^{(real)} - \bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)}$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule:** Retain factor $j$ if and only if:

$$\lambda_j^{adj} > 0 \iff \lambda_j^{(real)} > \bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:** Suppose $p = 5$, $B = 1000$, and the comparison yields:

| Factor $j$ | Real $\lambda_j$ | Random Mean $\bar{\lambda}_j$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.80 | 1.15 | Retain ✅ |
| 2 | 1.40 | 1.05 | Retain ✅ |
| 3 | 0.85 | 0.95 | Discard ❌ |
| 4 | 0.50 | 0.85 | Discard ❌ |
| 5 | 0.45 | 0.80 | Discard ❌ |

**Result:** $m = 2$ factors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stronger alternative:** Some authors use the 95th percentile of random eigenvalues rather than the mean, making the test more conservative. KTU examiners may expect either approach.

---

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Method | Sample Size | Variable Count | Subjectivity | Reliability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kaiser’s | Any | Small/Medium | None | Moderate |
| Scree Plot | Medium/Large | $p \geq 20$ | High | Moderate |
| Cumulative Variance | Any | Any | Low (threshold-based) | Moderate |
| Horn’s Parallel | Any | Any | None | **High** ⭐ |
| Velicer’s MAP | Medium/Large | Any | None | **High** ⭐ |
| BIC / AIC | Medium/Large | Any | None | **High** ⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow Diagram for Selecting the Number of Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Have correlation matrix R of p variables"] --> B["Step 1: Compute eigenvalues lambda_1 >= lambda_2 >= ... >= lambda_p"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Apply Kaiser's Criterion retain if lambda_j > 1"]
    C --> D{"Does m_kaiser match prior knowledge?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Use m_kaiser; verify with cumulative variance >= 70 percent"]
    D -- No --> F["Step 3: Construct Scree Plot and inspect elbow"]
    F --> G["Step 4: Compute Horn's Parallel Analysis with B = 1000"]
    G --> H{"Is m_horn > m_kaiser?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Reduce m to m_horn; run Velicer MAP test to confirm"]
    H -- No --> J["Step 5: Compute Velicer MAP for k = 0 to p-1"]
    J --> K["Step 6: Compute BIC and AIC for m = 1 to 5"]
    K --> L["Step 7: Cross-validate across methods"]
    L --> M{"Majority methods agree on m?"}
    M -- Yes --> N["Final m is the agreed value; proceed to factor rotation"]
    M -- No --> O["Use BIC minimum; or run sensitivity analysis with m +/- 1"]
    O --> N
    I --> J
    E --> N
```

### 4.2 Architectural Flow of the Horn’s Parallel Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Data X: n x p"] --> B["Compute Real Correlation Matrix R_real"]
    B --> C["Extract Eigenvalues: lambda_real"]
    C --> D["Initialize B = 1000 random datasets"]
    D --> E["For each permutation b: shuffle columns independently"]
    E --> F["Compute R_rand and lambda_rand"]
    F --> G["Average random eigenvalues across B permutations"]
    G --> H["Compare lambda_real with mean lambda_rand"]
    H --> I{"lambda_real_j > mean lambda_rand_j?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Retain factor j"]
    I -- No --> K["Discard factor j"]
    J --> L["Output: m final factors"]
    K --> L
```

### 4.3 Comparative Trade-off Schematic: Parsimony vs. Fit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Parsimony: fewer factors, simpler model"] --> Q["Trade-off Zone: optimal m"]
    R["Fit: more factors, captures all variance"] --> Q
    Q --> S["m = 2 to 4 for most social science data"]
    Q --> T["m = 5 to 10 for complex engineering sensor data"]
    Q --> U["m equals 1 for simple controlled experiments"]
```

### 4.4 Workflow Diagram: From Raw Data to Final Number of Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Data Matrix X n x p"] --> B["Step A: Standardization to Z-scores"]
    B --> C["Step B: Compute Correlation Matrix R"]
    C --> D["Step C: Eigenvalue Decomposition of R"]
    D --> E["Step D: Apply Six Methods in Parallel"]
    E --> F1["Kaiser Method"]
    E --> F2["Scree Plot"]
    E --> F3["Cumulative Variance"]
    E --> F4["Horn Parallel"]
    E --> F5["Velicer MAP"]
    E --> F6["BIC and AIC"]
    F1 --> G["Step E: Consensus across methods"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H["Final Decision: Optimal m"]
    H --> I["Step F: Estimate Loadings L via ML or PCA"]
    I --> J["Step G: Rotate factors Varimax or Promax"]
    J --> K["Step H: Interpret and Label factors"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers use the alphanumeric prefix rule (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) to comply with Mermaid parsing safeguards. Labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric to avoid markdown conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define the concept of "factor retention" in Exploratory Factor Analysis. List any two methods used for determining the number of factors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Factor retention refers to the decision step in EFA that determines the optimal number of common factors $m$ to be extracted from $p$ observed variables. The choice balances parsimony (simplicity) against adequate representation of the correlation structure. Two commonly used methods are:
1. **Kaiser’s Criterion:** Retain factors with eigenvalues $\lambda_j > 1$.
2. **Scree Plot:** Retain factors above the "elbow" of the eigenvalue plot.
*(Horn’s Parallel Analysis and Velicer’s MAP test are also valid answers.)* — **Award full 3 marks for any two correctly named methods with a clear definition.**

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **State and briefly explain Kaiser’s criterion for determining the number of factors. Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Kaiser’s criterion (1960) states that in factor analysis of a correlation matrix, only those common factors whose eigenvalues exceed **1.00** should be retained. The justification: a single standardized variable has unit variance; hence a factor explaining less than 1 unit of variance contributes less than a single variable and can be discarded. **Limitation:** The rule tends to **over-extract factors**, particularly when the number of variables $p$ is large ($p > 30$) or when the data deviate from multivariate normality, since sampling fluctuation alone can produce eigenvalues > 1 in random data. — **Award 2 marks for the rule + justification, and 1 mark for the limitation.**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain in detail the Scree Plot method for determining the number of factors. What are its advantages and disadvantages? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Scree Plot method was introduced by Raymond Cattell in 1966 as a graphical approach to factor retention.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Compute the correlation matrix $R$ of the $p$ standardized variables.
2. Perform eigenvalue decomposition to obtain ordered eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_p$.
3. Plot the eigenvalues $\lambda_j$ on the y-axis against the factor index $j$ on the x-axis.
4. The resulting curve is **monotonically decreasing** and typically has two distinct regions: a steep upper portion and a shallow lower tail.
5. The number of factors retained is the number of eigenvalues **above the "elbow"** — the point where the steep curve transitions into the shallow "scree" (the geological term for loose rubble at the base of a cliff).

**Mathematical Intuition (2 marks):** The steep portion represents factors that capture meaningful shared variance. The shallow scree represents factors that capture only trivial or noise variance.

**Advantages (0.5 marks):**
- Visually intuitive, especially for non-statistician researchers.
- Effective when $p \geq 20$ variables are available.

**Disadvantages (0.5 marks):**
- Highly **subjective**; different analysts may identify different elbows.
- Less reliable for small $p$ or when eigenvalues decay smoothly without a clear elbow.
- Does not provide a formal statistical test.

---

**(b) Describe Horn’s Parallel Analysis with its procedure. How is it superior to Kaiser’s criterion? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Horn’s Parallel Analysis (1965) is a Monte Carlo simulation-based method that compares the eigenvalues of the real data correlation matrix to those obtained from random data of the same size. It corrects the well-known **over-extraction bias** of Kaiser’s criterion by providing an empirical chance baseline.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Compute the real-data correlation matrix $R_{real}$ and its eigenvalues $\lambda_j^{(real)}$ for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$.
2. Perform $B$ iterations (typically $B = 1000$) where, in each iteration, generate a random dataset of the same dimensions $n \times p$ by **independently shuffling the columns** to destroy all inter-variable correlations.
3. For each iteration $b$, compute the correlation matrix $R_b^{(rand)}$ and extract its eigenvalues.
4. Compute the mean (or 95th percentile) of the random eigenvalues: $\bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \lambda_j^{(b,rand)}$.
5. Retain factor $j$ if and only if $\lambda_j^{(real)} > \bar{\lambda}_j^{(rand)}$.

**Why It Is Superior to Kaiser’s Criterion (2 marks):**
- **Accounts for sampling fluctuation:** Random data can produce eigenvalues > 1 simply by chance, especially when $p$ is large. Kaiser’s criterion retains these spuriously. Parallel analysis discards them.
- **Empirical, not arbitrary:** The threshold is data-driven, not a fixed value of 1.
- **Strong empirical support:** Studies (e.g., Zwick & Velicer, 1986) show Parallel Analysis has the lowest error rate among common retention methods.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the cumulative variance criterion for determining the number of factors. Take a suitable example with $p = 5$ variables and eigenvalues $\lambda = (2.8, 1.4, 0.7, 0.05, 0.05)$ to find the number of factors using a threshold of 75%. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (3 marks):** The cumulative variance criterion retains the minimum number of factors $m$ such that the cumulative proportion of total variance explained reaches a pre-specified threshold $T$. The standard threshold is **70%–80%** depending on the field.

**Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**

$$m = \min\left\{k : \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_j}{p} \geq T\right\}$$

**Worked Example (3 marks):** Given $p = 5$ and $\lambda = (2.8, 1.4, 0.7, 0.05, 0.05)$, and $T = 0.75$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} \lambda_j = 2.8 + 1.4 + 0.7 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 5.0 = p \quad \checkmark$$

**Compute cumulative proportions:**

$$C_1 = \frac{2.8}{5.0} = 0.56 \quad (< 0.75)$$

$$C_2 = \frac{2.8 + 1.4}{5.0} = \frac{4.2}{5.0} = 0.84 \quad (\geq 0.75) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The smallest $k$ for which $C_k \geq 0.75$ is $k = 2$. Therefore, **$m = 2$ factors** are retained.

---

**(b) Explain Velicer’s Minimum Average Partial (MAP) test for determining the number of factors. How does it differ from Kaiser’s criterion? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (3 marks):** Velicer’s MAP test (1976) is a non-graphical, statistically principled method that determines the optimal number of factors by minimizing the average squared partial correlation in the data after successively removing the influence of components.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Start with the original correlation matrix $R^{(0)} = R$.
2. For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, p - 1$:
   - Compute the matrix of partial correlations $r_{ij}^{(k)}$ after removing $k$ components.
   - Compute $f_k = \frac{\sum_{i \neq j} \left(r_{ij}^{(k)}\right)^2}{p(p-1)}$.
3. The optimal number of factors is $m = \arg\min_k f_k$.

**Intuition (1 mark):** The function $f_k$ measures the residual common variance in the data after $k$ factors have been partialed out. The minimum indicates that additional factors beyond $m$ are modeling noise.

**Difference from Kaiser’s Criterion (1 mark):**
- Kaiser uses a **fixed threshold** of 1 on eigenvalues; MAP uses a **data-driven minimum** of average partial correlation.
- Kaiser ignores inter-variable correlation structure after the first factor; MAP explicitly accounts for the full partial correlation matrix at each step.
- MAP is more robust to the number of variables $p$ and tends to extract fewer factors than Kaiser.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> 1. **Missing the "Why" in Kaiser’s Criterion (–1 mark):** Students often state "retain factors with $\lambda > 1$" but forget to justify *why* (because one standardized variable has unit variance). Always state the rationale.
> 2. **Confusing PCA and FA for factor number (–2 marks):** Kaiser's criterion on eigenvalues works identically in PCA and EFA on a correlation matrix, but the *interpretation* differs — in EFA, eigenvalues are used only for retention; factor loadings are then estimated separately. Examiners penalize answers that mix up these steps.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the threshold T in cumulative variance (–1 mark):** The threshold (70%, 75%, 80%) must be explicitly stated in any answer involving the cumulative variance method.
> 4. **Omitting the permutation count B in Horn’s analysis (–1 mark):** When describing Parallel Analysis, always mention $B = 1000$ or "large number of permutations" to be awarded full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the comparison with Kaiser in MAP answers (–1 mark):** KTU examiners specifically test whether students can critically compare methods. Always include a comparison paragraph.
> 6. **In numerical problems, not verifying $\sum \lambda_j = p$ (–0.5 marks):** This trace check is a quick way to confirm eigenvalue correctness and is rewarded in valuation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Determining the number of factors $m$ is the pre-extraction step in EFA that fixes the dimensionality of the latent space.
- **Factor Model Equation:** $X = \mu + LF + \epsilon$, with covariance $\Sigma = LL^T + \Psi$.
- **Kaiser’s Rule:** Retain factors with eigenvalue $\lambda_j > 1$. Threshold = **1.00**.
- **Cumulative Variance:** Retain smallest $m$ such that $C_m \geq T$ where $T \in [0.70, 0.80]$.
- **Scree Plot:** Retain factors above the "elbow" in the eigenvalue plot; subjective.
- **Horn’s Parallel Analysis:** Retain factors whose real eigenvalues exceed the mean of eigenvalues from $B = 1000$ permuted datasets. **Gold standard** for reliability.
- **Velicer’s MAP Test:** Retain $m = \arg\min_k f_k$ where $f_k$ is the average squared partial correlation after removing $k$ components.
- **BIC / AIC:** Retain $m$ that minimizes $BIC(m) = -2\ln L_m + q(m)\ln n$ with $q(m) = m(2p - m + 1)/2$.
- **Trace Identity:** $\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j = p$ for any correlation matrix.
- **Common Mistake:** Kaiser over-extracts; Parallel Analysis is the corrective remedy.
- **Practical Tip:** Always cross-validate using **at least two methods** before finalizing $m$.
- **Engineering Use:** Determines latent dimensions in sensor arrays, structural vibration, image processing, and biomedical signals.
- **Consensus Rule:** If methods disagree, prefer Horn’s Parallel Analysis or BIC; avoid relying solely on Kaiser’s criterion.
- **Scree Origin:** The word "scree" comes from geological term for rubble at the base of a mountain — Cattell’s analogy for the noise tail of the eigenvalue curve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 3 — Transformation of Factor Analysis Solutions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Factor Analysis**, the unrotated initial factor solution extracted via the **Principal Component Method** or **Principal Factor Method** does not generally yield factors that have a clear, simple, and substantively meaningful interpretation. To resolve this, statisticians apply a **linear transformation (rotation)** of the factor axes in the common-factor space such that the new loading matrix $\mathbf{L}^\star$ exhibits a **simple structure** — i.e., each variable loads highly on as few factors as possible, and each factor represents as few variables as possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformation of Factor Solutions** is the post-extraction rotation of the factor axes (orthogonal or oblique) to achieve a **simpler, more interpretable** factor pattern without changing the underlying communalities or the proportion of total variance explained.

The transformation is governed by a non-singular square matrix $\mathbf{T}$ of order $m \times m$ (where $m$ = number of retained factors), such that the rotated loading matrix is:

$$\mathbf{L}^\star = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbf{T}$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is the original $p \times m$ loading matrix and $\mathbf{L}^\star$ is the rotated loading matrix of the same dimensions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the center of a large room where **30 sensors** (variables) are scattered on the floor, each emitting a beep. You want to explain the data using just **3 hypothetical axes** (factors). The computer initially places these 3 axes at random, mathematical-convenient angles — but those axes don't line up with the natural "clusters" of sensors. As a result, every sensor has a moderate, mixed loading on every axis.

Now imagine **physically rotating the three axes together** so that one axis passes cleanly through Cluster A (temperature sensors), another through Cluster B (pressure sensors), and another through Cluster C (flow sensors). After the rotation, each sensor has a **high loading on exactly one axis** and **near-zero loadings on the others** — making the meaning of each factor instantly obvious.

> [!NOTE]
> The total "spread" of the data and the communalities of the variables **do not change** under orthogonal rotation — only the orientation of the axes changes. This is the statistical analogue of "rotating a 3-D coordinate system without stretching it."

### 1.3 Why Transformation is Required

The principal component / principal axis extraction maximizes **variance explained** (a mathematical criterion), not **psychological / engineering interpretability** (a scientific criterion). Hence, mathematical optimality ≠ interpretability. Rotation bridges this gap.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** "Rotation = Repositioning axes → Simple Structure → Interpretation."

### 1.4 Key Terms (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Loading** | Correlation (or regression weight) of a variable with a factor. |
| **Simple Structure** | Thurstone's criterion: each row & column of $\mathbf{L}^\star$ has few significant entries. |
| **Orthogonal Rotation** | Axes remain at right angles (factors uncorrelated). |
| **Oblique Rotation** | Axes need not remain perpendicular (factors allowed to correlate). |
| **Factor Score** | Estimated value of a factor for an individual case. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Objective of Rotation

Given $\mathbf{L}$ of order $p \times m$, find a non-singular $\mathbf{T}$ of order $m \times m$ such that $\mathbf{L}^\star = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}$ satisfies a chosen **simplicity criterion** $C(\mathbf{L}^\star)$.

Two broad families:

1. **Orthogonal Rotations** — $\mathbf{T}^\top \mathbf{T} = \mathbf{I}_m$. Factor correlation matrix remains $\mathbf{I}$.
2. **Oblique Rotations** — $\mathbf{T}$ is general non-singular; the new factors are allowed to correlate, producing a factor correlation matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \neq \mathbf{I}$.

### 2.2 Thurstone's Five Conditions for Simple Structure

1. Each factor should have a **zero loading** on at least $m$ variables.
2. Each factor should have **non-zero loadings** on at least $m$ variables.
3. For every pair of factors, **most variables** should have zero loading on one factor and non-zero on the other.
4. For every pair of factors, the **majority of variables** should have small / negligible loadings on both.
5. For every pair of factors, **no variable** should have high loadings on both.

The closer $\mathbf{L}^\star$ is to satisfying these, the more "interpretable" it is.

### 2.3 Orthogonal Rotation Methods

#### 2.3.1 Varimax Rotation (Kaiser, 1958) — *Most popular in KTU problems*

The orthogonal criterion that **maximizes the variance of the squared loadings within each column** of $\mathbf{L}^\star$ (column-wise simplicity). Denote $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \ell_{ij}^{\star\,2}$ as the communality of variable $i$.

Define the **squared loading normalized by communality**:

$$\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^{\star} = \frac{\ell_{ij}^{\star}}{\sqrt{h_i^2}}$$

The **Varimax criterion** is:

$$V = \sum_{j=1}^{m}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{4} - \frac{1}{p}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\right)^{2}\right]$$

Equivalently, per-factor variance:

$$V_j = p\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{4} - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\right)^{2}$$

The full criterion $V = \sum_{j=1}^{m} V_j$ is maximized by iterative plane-by-plane 2-factor rotations using the closed-form angle $\phi$ in the $(a,b)$ plane:

$$\tan(4\phi) = \frac{D - 2AB}{C - (A^2 - B^2)}$$

where:

$$u_i = \bigl(\tilde{\ell}_{ia}^{\star}\bigr)^2 - \bigl(\tilde{\ell}_{ib}^{\star}\bigr)^2,\qquad v_i = 2\,\tilde{\ell}_{ia}^{\star}\,\tilde{\ell}_{ib}^{\star}$$

$$A = \sum_i u_i,\quad B = \sum_i v_i,\quad C = \sum_i (u_i^2 - v_i^2),\quad D = 2\sum_i u_i v_i$$

#### 2.3.2 Quartimax Rotation

Maximizes the **sum of fourth powers of all loadings**:

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{4}$$

Tends to produce a **general factor** (one big factor + small bipolars).

#### 2.3.3 Equimax Rotation

A compromise between Varimax and Quartimax:

$$E = \sum_{j=1}^{m}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{4} - \frac{\gamma}{p}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\right)^{2}\right]$$

Setting $\gamma = m/2$ (a constant for a given problem) gives the **Equimax** compromise. (Quartimax = $\gamma=0$; Varimax = $\gamma=1$.)

#### 2.3.4 Generalized Orthomax Criterion

A single unifying family:

$$G = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{4} - \frac{\gamma}{p}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\right)^{2}$$

| $\gamma$ value | Method |
|----|---------|
| $\gamma = 1$ | Varimax |
| $\gamma = 0$ | Quartimax |
| $\gamma = m/2$ | Equimax |
| $0 < \gamma < 1$ | Generalized orthomax |

### 2.4 Oblique Rotation Methods

When factors are theoretically expected to be **correlated** (e.g., "anxiety" and "stress" factors in psychology), orthogonal rotation is overly restrictive. Oblique rotations allow the factor axes to depart from perpendicularity.

#### 2.4.1 Oblimin Rotation

Minimizes:

$$\Delta = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\sum_{j<k}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star\,2}\,\ell_{ik}^{\star\,2}\bigr) - \gamma\,\frac{p(p-1)}{2}\sum_{j<k}\!\operatorname{sgn}\!\bigl(\rho_{jk}\bigr)$$

Equivalently (primary form), we minimize:

$$\Delta = \sum_{j<k}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\bigl(\ell_{ik}^{\star}\bigr)^{2} - \frac{\gamma}{p}\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\ell_{ij}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\Bigr)\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\bigl(\ell_{ik}^{\star}\bigr)^{2}\Bigr)\right]$$

The parameter $\gamma$ controls obliqueness (typically $\gamma \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1\}$). For $\gamma = 0$ we get **Oblimin (Covarimin)**. Higher negative values yield more oblique solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Oblique rotations yield **two matrices**: the **Pattern Matrix** $\mathbf{P}$ (loadings on oblique factors) and the **Structure Matrix** $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ (correlations of variables with factors).

#### 2.4.2 Promax Rotation (Hendrickson & White, 1964)

A computationally fast **oblique method**:
1. Perform a **Varimax rotation** to get $\mathbf{L}_V$.
2. Raise each column $j$ of $\mathbf{L}_V$ to a power $k$ (typically $k=2,3,4$) to create a target matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with elements $k_{ij} = \operatorname{sgn}(\ell_{V,ij})\,\bigl(\vert\ell_{V,ij}\vert\bigr)^{k+1}$.
3. Find the **Procrustes rotation** $\mathbf{T}$ that best maps $\mathbf{L}_V$ to $\mathbf{K}$ in the least-squares sense.
4. Rescale $\mathbf{T}$ to obtain the oblique solution.

### 2.5 Factor Scores

Once a final rotated solution $\mathbf{L}^\star$ (or $\mathbf{P}$) is chosen, we estimate the value of each factor $F_j$ for observation $i$ via:

**Regression Method (Thomson / Bartlett):**

$$\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \mathbf{Z}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{L}^\star\,\bigl(\mathbf{L}^{\star\,\top}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\star\bigr)^{-1}$$

where $\mathbf{Z}$ is the $n \times p$ standardized data matrix and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correlation matrix.

For orthogonal factors with $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^\top + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \mathbf{Z}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{L}^\star\,\operatorname{diag}\!\bigl(\mathbf{L}^{\star\,\top}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\star\bigr)^{-1/2}$$

For oblique factors with $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{P}^\top + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \mathbf{Z}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{P}\,\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{-1}$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Definition | Used For |
|----|------|------|------|
| 1 | Rotated loadings | $\mathbf{L}^\star = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}$ | All rotations |
| 2 | Orthogonal condition | $\mathbf{T}^\top \mathbf{T} = \mathbf{I}$ | Varimax / Quartimax / Equimax |
| 3 | Communality invariance | $h_i^{2\,\star} = h_i^{2}$ | Verifying rotation validity |
| 4 | Varimax criterion | $V = \sum_j [p\sum_i (\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^\star)^4 - (\sum_i (\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^\star)^2)^2]$ | Maximize for simplicity |
| 5 | Quartimax criterion | $Q = \sum_i \sum_j (\ell_{ij}^\star)^4$ | General-factor preference |
| 6 | Generalized orthomax | $G = \sum\sum (\ell_{ij}^\star)^4 - \frac{\gamma}{p}\sum_j (\sum_i (\ell_{ij}^\star)^2)^2$ | Unifying family |
| 7 | Structure matrix | $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ | Oblique rotations |
| 8 | Factor scores (Bartlett) | $\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \mathbf{Z}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\star(\mathbf{L}^{\star\,\top}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\star)^{-1}$ | Score estimation |
| 9 | Factor correlation | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ (oblique) / $\mathbf{I}$ (orthogonal) | Checking dependence |
| 10 | Promax target | $k_{ij} = \operatorname{sgn}(\ell_{V,ij})\,\bigl(\vert\ell_{V,ij}\vert\bigr)^{k+1}$ | Oblique target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Rotation does **NOT** change communalities, total variance explained, or the underlying correlation matrix — only the **axes** are repositioned. A common KTU student error is to claim that rotation alters $h_i^2$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Signal Processing:** Rotating factor axes is mathematically equivalent to rotating a sensor basis in ICA/PCA blind-source separation — used in EEG, speech, and vibration analysis.
- **Quality Engineering:** Multivariate SPC dashboards use rotated factor scores to detect abnormal process behaviour.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene-expression factor analysis uses Oblimin / Promax to discover correlated gene-modules (pathways).
- **Materials Science:** Reducing spectral/structural data to interpretable latent factors (e.g., phase components).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Orthogonal Rotation Preserves Communalities

**Goal:** Prove $h_i^{2\,\star} = h_i^{2}$ for any orthogonal $\mathbf{T}$.

Let $\boldsymbol{\ell}_i^\top$ be the $i$-th row of $\mathbf{L}$ (loading vector of variable $i$ across the $m$ factors). Then $h_i^2 = \boldsymbol{\ell}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\ell}_i$.

After orthogonal rotation:

$$\boldsymbol{\ell}_i^{\star\,\top} = \boldsymbol{\ell}_i^\top \mathbf{T}$$

Therefore:

$$h_i^{2\,\star} = \boldsymbol{\ell}_i^{\star\,\top}\boldsymbol{\ell}_i^{\star} = \boldsymbol{\ell}_i^\top \mathbf{T}\mathbf{T}^\top \boldsymbol{\ell}_i$$

Using the orthogonal condition $\mathbf{T}\mathbf{T}^\top = \mathbf{I}_m$:

$$h_i^{2\,\star} = \boldsymbol{\ell}_i^\top \mathbf{I}_m \boldsymbol{\ell}_i = \boldsymbol{\ell}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\ell}_i = h_i^{2}$$

**Result:** Communalities are invariant under any orthogonal rotation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Form Varimax 2-Factor Rotation Angle

**Goal:** Derive $\tan(4\phi)$ that maximizes $V_{ab}$ in the plane of factors $a$ and $b$.

Consider only two factors $a$ and $b$. The pair of rows of $\mathbf{L}^\star$ after rotating these two columns by an angle $\phi$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ell_{ia}^{\star}(\phi) &= \ell_{ia}\cos\phi + \ell_{ib}\sin\phi \\
\ell_{ib}^{\star}(\phi) &= -\ell_{ia}\sin\phi + \ell_{ib}\cos\phi
\end{aligned}$$

Communalities don't change (orthogonal), so we can directly define normalized quantities:

$$x_i = \ell_{ia},\quad y_i = \ell_{ib},\quad u_i = x_i^2 - y_i^2,\quad v_i = 2 x_i y_i$$

After rotation:

$$(x_i^\star)^2 - (y_i^\star)^2 = u_i \cos(2\phi) + v_i \sin(2\phi)$$
$$2 x_i^\star y_i^\star = -u_i \sin(2\phi) + v_i \cos(2\phi)$$

The Varimax criterion restricted to factors $a,b$:

$$V_{ab} = p\sum_i (x_i^\star)^4 + p\sum_i (y_i^\star)^4 - \left[\sum_i (x_i^\star)^2\right]^2 - \left[\sum_i (y_i^\star)^2\right]^2$$

Setting $\dfrac{dV_{ab}}{d\phi} = 0$ and simplifying (using $\dfrac{d}{d\phi}(z^2) = 2z\dfrac{dz}{d\phi}$ and identities for double-angle), we obtain:

$$\tan(4\phi) = \frac{D - 2AB}{C - (A^2 - B^2)}$$

where

$$A = \sum_i u_i,\quad B = \sum_i v_i,\quad C = \sum_i (u_i^2 - v_i^2),\quad D = 2\sum_i u_i v_i$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Varimax Rotation of a 2-Factor Loading Matrix

**Given:** Loading matrix

$$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.80 & 0.60 \\ 0.85 & 0.55 \\ 0.30 & 0.90 \\ 0.25 & 0.95 \end{bmatrix},\qquad p = 4,\ m = 2$$

Variables 1,2 load moderately on both factors; variables 3,4 also load on both. We apply Varimax.

**Step 1 — Compute communalities.**

$$h_1^2 = 0.80^2 + 0.60^2 = 1.00$$
$$h_2^2 = 0.85^2 + 0.55^2 = 1.00$$
$$h_3^2 = 0.30^2 + 0.90^2 = 0.90$$
$$h_4^2 = 0.25^2 + 0.95^2 = 0.965$$

**Step 2 — Normalize loadings** by $\sqrt{h_i^2}$:

$$\tilde{x}_i = \frac{\ell_{i1}}{\sqrt{h_i^2}},\quad \tilde{y}_i = \frac{\ell_{i2}}{\sqrt{h_i^2}}$$

$$\tilde{x}_1 = 0.800,\quad \tilde{y}_1 = 0.600$$
$$\tilde{x}_2 = 0.850,\quad \tilde{y}_2 = 0.550$$
$$\tilde{x}_3 = 0.316,\quad \tilde{y}_3 = 0.949$$
$$\tilde{x}_4 = 0.254,\quad \tilde{y}_4 = 0.967$$

**Step 3 — Compute $u_i, v_i$.**

| $i$ | $u_i = \tilde{x}_i^2 - \tilde{y}_i^2$ | $v_i = 2\tilde{x}_i\tilde{y}_i$ |
|----|------|------|
| 1 | $0.64 - 0.36 = 0.28$ | $2(0.800)(0.600) = 0.960$ |
| 2 | $0.7225 - 0.3025 = 0.420$ | $2(0.850)(0.550) = 0.935$ |
| 3 | $0.100 - 0.901 = -0.801$ | $2(0.316)(0.949) = 0.600$ |
| 4 | $0.0645 - 0.935 = -0.871$ | $2(0.254)(0.967) = 0.491$ |
| $\sum$ | $A = -0.972$ | $B = 2.986$ |

**Step 4 — Compute $u_i^2 - v_i^2$ and $2u_i v_i$.**

| $i$ | $u_i^2 - v_i^2$ | $2u_i v_i$ |
|----|------|------|
| 1 | $0.0784 - 0.9216 = -0.843$ | $2(0.28)(0.960) = 0.538$ |
| 2 | $0.1764 - 0.8742 = -0.698$ | $2(0.420)(0.935) = 0.785$ |
| 3 | $0.642 - 0.360 = 0.282$ | $2(-0.801)(0.600) = -0.961$ |
| 4 | $0.759 - 0.241 = 0.518$ | $2(-0.871)(0.491) = -0.855$ |
| $\sum$ | $C = -0.741$ | $D = -0.493$ |

**Step 5 — Compute $\tan(4\phi)$.**

$$\text{Numerator} = D - 2AB = -0.493 - 2(-0.972)(2.986) = -0.493 + 5.806 = 5.313$$

$$\text{Denominator} = C - (A^2 - B^2) = -0.741 - (0.945 - 8.916) = -0.741 + 7.971 = 7.230$$

$$\tan(4\phi) = \frac{5.313}{7.230} = 0.735$$

$$4\phi = \arctan(0.735) = 36.30^\circ \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \phi \approx 9.08^\circ$$

**Step 6 — Build rotation matrix** $\mathbf{T}$:

$$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & \sin\phi \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9876 & 0.1578 \\ -0.1578 & 0.9876 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Compute rotated loadings** $\mathbf{L}^\star = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}$.

Row 1: $(0.80,0.60)\mathbf{T} = (0.80\cdot0.9876 + 0.60\cdot(-0.1578),\ 0.80\cdot0.1578 + 0.60\cdot0.9876)$
$\;= (0.7901 - 0.0947,\ 0.1262 + 0.5926) = (0.6954,\ 0.7188)$

Row 2: $(0.85,0.55)\mathbf{T} = (0.85\cdot0.9876 - 0.55\cdot0.1578,\ 0.85\cdot0.1578 + 0.55\cdot0.9876)$
$\;= (0.8395 - 0.0868,\ 0.1341 + 0.5432) = (0.7527,\ 0.6773)$

Row 3: $(0.30,0.90)\mathbf{T} = (0.30\cdot0.9876 - 0.90\cdot0.1578,\ 0.30\cdot0.1578 + 0.90\cdot0.9876)$
$\;= (0.2963 - 0.1420,\ 0.0473 + 0.8888) = (0.1543,\ 0.9361)$

Row 4: $(0.25,0.95)\mathbf{T} = (0.25\cdot0.9876 - 0.95\cdot0.1578,\ 0.25\cdot0.1578 + 0.95\cdot0.9876)$
$\;= (0.2469 - 0.1499,\ 0.0394 + 0.9382) = (0.0970,\ 0.9776)$

$$\mathbf{L}^\star \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.695 & 0.719 \\ 0.753 & 0.677 \\ 0.154 & 0.936 \\ 0.097 & 0.978 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Verify simple structure improvement.**

Before rotation, every variable loaded moderately on both factors (no clear pattern). After Varimax, variables 1 & 2 still load on both, but variables 3 & 4 now load **predominantly** on Factor 2. The Varimax rotation has begun to differentiate the factors. Repeating the iterative cycle (next plane rotation) would sharpen this further.

**Step 9 — Verify communality invariance.**

$h_1^{2\,\star} = 0.695^2 + 0.719^2 = 0.483 + 0.517 = 1.000 = h_1^2$ ✓

$h_3^{2\,\star} = 0.154^2 + 0.936^2 = 0.0237 + 0.8762 = 0.900 = h_3^2$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the communality invariance check — it is a standard validation step examiners expect for full marks.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Rotation

| Situation | Recommended Rotation | Reason |
|-----------|--------------------|--------|
| Factors theoretically uncorrelated (e.g., distinct physical dimensions) | **Varimax** | Most widely used; maximizes column simplicity. |
| Seeking one general factor dominating | **Quartimax** | Concentrates variance on first factor. |
| Compromise between Varimax and Quartimax | **Equimax** | Balanced simplicity. |
| Factors known / expected to correlate (e.g., cognitive abilities) | **Promax** (fast) or **Oblimin** (theory-driven) | Allows correlated factors. |
| Exploratory analysis, unknown factor relations | **Varimax first**, then Promax if needed | Common KTU workflow. |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Factor Rotation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Matrix X of order n x p] --> B[Standardization Z]
    B --> C[Correlation Matrix R]
    C --> D[Factor Extraction PCA / PFA]
    D --> E[Initial Loading Matrix L]
    E --> F{Choice of Rotation}
    F --> G1[Varimax]
    F --> G2[Quartimax]
    F --> G3[Equimax]
    F --> G4[Promax Oblique]
    F --> G5[Oblimin Oblique]
    G1 --> H1[Transformation T orthogonal]
    G2 --> H2[Transformation T orthogonal]
    G3 --> H3[Transformation T orthogonal]
    G4 --> H4[Target Matrix K then Procrustes]
    G5 --> H5[Minimization Delta with gamma]
    H1 --> I[Rotated Loadings L star]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> J[Pattern Matrix P and Phi]
    H5 --> J
    I --> K[Interpret Factors]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Factor Scores F hat]
    L --> M[Downstream Analysis SPC Clustering Regression]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Rotation in 2-D Factor Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Factor 1 axis] -->|rotate by phi| C[New Factor 1 axis]
    B[Original Factor 2 axis] -->|rotate by phi| D[New Factor 2 axis]
    A2[Variable V1 load on F1 high load on F2 moderate] --> E[After rotation high on F1star low on F2star]
    B2[Variable V2 load on F1 moderate load on F2 high] --> F[After rotation low on F1star high on F2star]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Rotation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotation of Factor Solutions] --> B[Orthogonal T transpose T = I]
    A --> C[Oblique T general non singular]
    B --> B1[Quartimax gamma = 0]
    B --> B2[Varimax gamma = 1]
    B --> B3[Equimax gamma = m / 2]
    B --> B4[Generalized Orthomax 0 to 1]
    C --> C1[Oblimin gamma parameter]
    C --> C2[Promax target Procrustes]
    C --> C3[Covarimin gamma = 0]
    C --> C4[Quartimin]
```

### 4.4 Workflow — Simple Structure Verification Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Compute V criterion for current L star] --> S2{Converged}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Pick pair of factors a b]
    S3 --> S4[Compute closed form angle phi]
    S4 --> S5[Apply 2 factor rotation update]
    S5 --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S6[Report final rotated L star and factor scores]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define simple structure in factor analysis and list any three conditions proposed by Thurstone.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Simple Structure** is an ideal pattern of factor loadings in which each variable loads highly on as few factors as possible and each factor is defined by as few variables as possible, making the factors interpretable. Three of Thurstone's five conditions are:
1. Each factor should have zero loadings on at least $m$ variables.
2. Each factor should have non-zero loadings on at least $m$ variables.
3. For every pair of factors, the majority of variables should have small / negligible loadings on both.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**
*Distinguish between orthogonal and oblique rotation in factor analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Orthogonal | Oblique |
|--------|------------|---------|
| Axis angle | Axes remain at 90° | Axes free to depart from 90° |
| Condition on $\mathbf{T}$ | $\mathbf{T}^\top\mathbf{T}=\mathbf{I}$ | $\mathbf{T}$ non-singular (no constraint) |
| Factor correlation | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}=\mathbf{I}$ (uncorrelated) | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}\neq\mathbf{I}$ (correlated allowed) |
| Output matrix | Single $\mathbf{L}^\star$ | Pattern $\mathbf{P}$ and Structure $\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ |
| Methods | Varimax, Quartimax, Equimax | Promax, Oblimin |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Varimax rotation criterion**. Derive the closed-form expression for the rotation angle $\phi$ in the two-factor case. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given the loading matrix below for $p=4$ variables and $m=2$ factors, perform one iteration of the Varimax rotation. Compute the rotated loadings and verify that communalities are preserved. (7 marks)

$$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.80 & 0.60 \\ 0.85 & 0.55 \\ 0.30 & 0.90 \\ 0.25 & 0.95 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the Varimax criterion (2 marks).** The Varimax criterion maximizes the sum over factors of the variance of squared normalized loadings within each column:

$$V = \sum_{j=1}^{m}\left[p\sum_{i=1}^{p}(\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^\star)^4 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p}(\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^\star)^2\right)^2\right]$$

where $\tilde{\ell}_{ij}^\star = \ell_{ij}^\star / \sqrt{h_i^2}$.

**Step 2 — Restrict to two factors $a,b$ and define $u_i,v_i$ (2 marks).**

$$u_i = (\tilde{\ell}_{ia})^2 - (\tilde{\ell}_{ib})^2,\quad v_i = 2\,\tilde{\ell}_{ia}\tilde{\ell}_{ib}$$

**Step 3 — Rotation formulas (2 marks).**

$$x_i^\star = \ell_{ia}\cos\phi + \ell_{ib}\sin\phi,\quad y_i^\star = -\ell_{ia}\sin\phi + \ell_{ib}\cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and solve (1 mark).** Setting $\dfrac{dV_{ab}}{d\phi}=0$ and using double-angle identities:

$$\tan(4\phi) = \frac{D - 2AB}{C - (A^2 - B^2)}$$

with $A=\sum u_i,\ B=\sum v_i,\ C=\sum(u_i^2-v_i^2),\ D=2\sum u_iv_i$.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

The full step-by-step computation is shown in **Section 3.3** of these notes. Key results:

$$\phi \approx 9.08^\circ,\quad \mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix}0.9876 & 0.1578\\-0.1578 & 0.9876\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{L}^\star \approx \begin{bmatrix}0.695 & 0.719\\0.753 & 0.677\\0.154 & 0.936\\0.097 & 0.978\end{bmatrix}$$

Communalities preserved: $h_1^2=h_2^2=1.000,\ h_3^2=0.900,\ h_4^2=0.965$ (identical before and after). ✓

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Describe the **Promax rotation** method for obtaining oblique factor solutions. How does it differ from Oblimin? (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the difference between the **pattern matrix $\mathbf{P}$** and the **structure matrix $\mathbf{S}$** in oblique rotation. Given an oblique pattern $\mathbf{P}$ and factor correlation $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$, write the expression for $\mathbf{S}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Promax Rotation (Hendrickson & White, 1964) — Procedure (5 marks):**
1. Start with the **Varimax-rotated orthogonal loading matrix** $\mathbf{L}_V$ (this ensures a clean reference orientation).
2. Construct a **target matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ by raising the absolute loadings of $\mathbf{L}_V$ to a power $(k+1)$, typically $k = 2, 3,$ or $4$:
$$k_{ij} = \operatorname{sgn}(\ell_{V,ij})\,\bigl(\vert\ell_{V,ij}\vert\bigr)^{k+1}$$
The sign-preserving power amplifies large loadings and shrinks small ones, creating an idealized "simple-structure" target.
3. Find a **Procrustes transformation** $\mathbf{T}_P$ that maps $\mathbf{L}_V$ to $\mathbf{K}$ in the least-squares sense:
$$\mathbf{T}_P = (\mathbf{L}_V^\top\mathbf{L}_V)^{-1}\mathbf{L}_V^\top\mathbf{K}$$
4. **Rescale** columns of $\mathbf{T}_P$ so that $\mathbf{T}_P^\top\mathbf{L}_V^\top\mathbf{L}_V\mathbf{T}_P = \operatorname{diag}$ (each column unit-variance).
5. The oblique pattern matrix is $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{L}_V \mathbf{T}_P$ (rescaled). The factor correlation matrix is $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \propto (\mathbf{P}^\top\mathbf{P})^{-1}$.

**Comparison with Oblimin (2 marks):** Oblimin is a **direct minimization** method that minimizes an explicit criterion $\Delta$ involving cross-products of squared loadings, controlled by the $\gamma$ parameter (typically $-2$ to $+1$). Promax is a **two-stage, computationally cheap heuristic** using Varimax as a base; Oblimin requires iterative optimization at every $\gamma$.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Pattern vs Structure — Conceptual (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Pattern Matrix $\mathbf{P}$ | Structure Matrix $\mathbf{S}$ |
|--------|------|------|
| Definition | Unique regression weights of variables on oblique factors | Correlations of variables with oblique factors |
| Diagonal property | Generally $\mathbf{P}^\top\mathbf{P}$ ≠ correlation | $\mathbf{S}$ has the same column variances as $\mathbf{P}$ via $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ |
| Element meaning | "If I unit-change a factor, how does the variable change?" | "How strongly does the variable correlate with the factor?" |
| When to use | Naming and interpreting factors | Describing variables |

**Mathematical relation (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is the $m \times m$ factor correlation matrix. For orthogonal factors, $\boldsymbol{\Phi}=\mathbf{I}$, so $\mathbf{S}=\mathbf{P}$.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A simple block diagram shows variables $\rightarrow$ oblique factors, with factor correlation $\phi_{12}$ linking the two oblique axes (drawn at angle $\theta \neq 90^\circ$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Pattern with Structure** in oblique rotation — examiners frequently test this. *Pattern* = regression coefficient on oblique factor; *Structure* = correlation.
> 2. **Don't claim rotation changes communalities** — they remain invariant under orthogonal rotation (this is a free 1-mark question).
> 3. **Don't use a single $\gamma$ value as "the answer"** for Oblimin — always state that $\gamma$ controls the degree of obliqueness and is data-driven.
> 4. **Always normalize loadings by $\sqrt{h_i^2}$** before applying the Varimax formula; skipping this is a common 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Don't forget the communality-invariance check** in numerical problems — it confirms correct application and earns easy marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Transformation Equation:** $\mathbf{L}^\star = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}$. This is the **single most important** equation in the topic.
- **Orthogonal → $\mathbf{T}^\top\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{I}$.** Oblique → no constraint, but **two output matrices** appear: $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$.
- **Communalities & total variance are invariant** under orthogonal rotation. (Property used as a verification step in numericals.)
- **Varimax criterion** $V$ maximizes column-wise variance of squared normalized loadings; **Quartimax** $Q$ maximizes sum of fourth powers; **Equimax** is a $\gamma$-compromise.
- **Closed-form 2-factor rotation angle:** $\tan(4\phi) = \dfrac{D - 2AB}{C - (A^2 - B^2)}$ where $A,B,C,D$ are sums of $u_i, v_i, u_i^2 - v_i^2, 2u_i v_i$.
- **Oblique Methods:** Promax (fast, two-stage, target-based) and Oblimin (direct minimization with parameter $\gamma$).
- **Factor Scores (Bartlett/Regression):** $\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\star(\mathbf{L}^{\star\top}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\star)^{-1}$ for orthogonal; multiply by $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{-1}$ on right for oblique.
- **KTU workflow:** Extract → Rotate (Varimax first) → Interpret → Score → Use.
- **Simple structure (Thurstone)** is the **interpretability criterion** that rotation aims to achieve.
- **Decision rule:** Use orthogonal rotations when factors are theoretically independent; use oblique when theory or data suggest correlation among factors.
- **Output naming:** For orthogonal, the rotated matrix is "Loadings $\mathbf{L}^\star$". For oblique, you must report **Pattern $\mathbf{P}$, Structure $\mathbf{S}$, and Factor Correlation $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$** — all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor Scores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT503 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factor Scores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Factor Scores):** *Factor scores* are the numerically estimated values of the unobserved (latent) common factors for each individual case (observation) in a dataset, derived from the observed standardized variables via the inverse of the **Factor Analysis** model. They are computed using a **Factor Score Coefficient Matrix** (also called the *weight matrix* or *regression coefficient matrix*).

In matrix notation, if $Z$ is the $n \times p$ matrix of standardized observations, $A$ is the $p \times m$ factor loading matrix ($m < p$), $\hat{F}$ is the $n \times m$ matrix of estimated factor scores, and $W$ is the $p \times m$ factor score coefficient matrix, then:

$$\hat{F} = Z W$$

The choice of $W$ depends on the estimation method (Regression, Bartlett, Anderson–Rubin, etc.), each of which optimizes a different statistical criterion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a survey with **20 questions** designed to measure two hidden traits in people — say, **"Introversion"** and **"Mathematical Aptitude"**. These two traits (factors) are *not directly measured*. They are *latent* (hidden).

You can't give someone a score of "Introversion = 6.8" by asking just one question. But based on the **pattern of all 20 answers**, you can *guess* their score on the hidden trait. This guessed value is the **factor score**.

Think of it this way:

| Real-world object | Statistical equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hidden personality trait | Latent common factor ($F$) |
| Survey answer sheet | Observed variables ($X$ or $Z$) |
| The "best guess" of the trait level | Factor score ($\hat{F}$) |
| Recipe for cooking the guess from answers | Factor score coefficient matrix ($W$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Factor scores are *estimates*, not exact values. Different estimation methods (recipes) will give slightly different factor scores for the same individual, but all attempt to produce the "best" estimate based on a chosen optimality criterion.

## 1.3 Why Factor Scores Matter in KTU Computational Statistics

In KTU's PCCBT503 framework, factor scores are essential because they:

- Convert an **abstract factor model** into **usable numerical data** for downstream analysis.
- Enable **regression, clustering, or classification** using the reduced factor representation.
- Form the **bridge** between exploratory factor analysis (EFA) and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) applications.

The **standard metrics** relevant to factor score estimation include:

- **Communalities** ($h_i^2$) — variance of variable $i$ explained by the common factors.
- **Specific variance** ($\psi_i$) — variance unique to variable $i$.
- **Eigenvalue criterion** (Kaiser: $\lambda \geq 1$) — used to select $m$ factors before computing scores.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Framework

Recall the **orthogonal factor model** for the standardized variable vector $Z = (Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_p)^T$:

$$Z = AF + U$$

where:
- $A$ is a $p \times m$ matrix of **factor loadings**,
- $F = (F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_m)^T$ is the vector of **common factors** (with $E[F] = 0$, $\text{Cov}(F) = I_m$),
- $U$ is the $p \times 1$ vector of **specific (unique) factors** (with $E[U] = 0$, $\text{Cov}(U) = \Psi$ a diagonal matrix).

The covariance structure is:

$$\Sigma = AA^T + \Psi$$

Since $F$ and $U$ are orthogonal by assumption, we are in the **uncorrelated factor** setting.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Factor Scores

A single respondent never explicitly answers the factor $F_j$. We observe only $Z$ (or $X$). The aim is to find a linear function:

$$\hat{F}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{p} w_{ij} Z_i = W_j^T Z$$

such that the *regression of $F_j$ on $Z$* (which is the **best linear predictor** in the mean-squared-error sense) is approximated. Since $F$ is unobserved, different criteria give different $W$.

> [!NOTE]
> The best *true* predictor in the L2 sense is $E[F \mid Z]$, but this requires the joint distribution. Factor score methods approximate this using **least-squares ideas applied to the moment matrix** $\Sigma$.

## 2.3 The Four Principal Estimation Methods

### 2.3.1 Regression (Thomson) Method

Minimizes the sum of squared residuals in predicting $F$ from $Z$. Uses the population regression formula:

$$W_R = A^T (AA^T + \Psi)^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $W_R$ is exactly the coefficient matrix of the linear regression of the latent $F$ on the observed $Z$. It is the most commonly used method and yields scores that are **biased but consistent** in the factor model.

### 2.3.2 Bartlett Method

Maximizes the sum of squared standardized residuals, giving:

$$W_B = (A^T \Psi^{-1} A)^{-1} A^T \Psi^{-1}$$

This produces factor scores that are **standardized** (variance = 1) under the model.

### 2.3.3 Anderson–Rubin Method

Modified to ensure $\hat{F}$ is **uncorrelated** (orthogonal), by using:

$$W_{AR} = (A^T \Psi^{-1} A)^{-1/2} A^T \Psi^{-1}$$

(where the matrix square root is taken of the symmetric positive-definite $A^T \Psi^{-1} A$). The resulting scores satisfy $\frac{1}{n} \hat{F}^T \hat{F} = I_m$ exactly.

### 2.3.4 Ten Berge Method

A trimmed compromise; preserves the regression interpretation while ensuring correlation-structure fidelity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Property |
|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | Standardized observation vector ($n \times p$) | $Z = (X - \mu) \Sigma^{-1/2}$ |
| $A$ | Factor loading matrix ($p \times m$) | Rows = loadings of variable on $m$ factors |
| $\Psi$ | Specific variance matrix ($p \times p$) | Diagonal; $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ |
| $h_i^2$ | Communality of variable $i$ | $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{m} a_{ij}^2$ |
| $\Sigma$ | Population covariance | $\Sigma = AA^T + \Psi$ |
| $R$ | Sample correlation matrix | Used as $\Sigma$ estimate in practice |
| $\hat{F}$ | Estimated factor score matrix ($n \times m$) | $\hat{F} = Z W$ |
| $W_R$ | Regression weight matrix | $A^T(AA^T + \Psi)^{-1}$ |
| $W_B$ | Bartlett weight matrix | $(A^T \Psi^{-1} A)^{-1} A^T \Psi^{-1}$ |
| $W_{AR}$ | Anderson–Rubin weight matrix | $(A^T \Psi^{-1} A)^{-1/2} A^T \Psi^{-1}$ |
| $E[\hat{F}_R]$ | Mean of regression scores | $\mathbf{0}$ |
| $\text{Cov}(\hat{F}_R)$ | Cov of regression scores | $A^T(AA^T+\Psi)^{-1} A$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Factor scores are used in:
