## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — K-Means Algorithm and Profiling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Dataset D with n points and p features] --> std[Step 0: Standardize Features using Z-score]
    std --> ksel[Step 1: Choose k using Elbow or Silhouette]
    ksel --> init[Step 2: Initialize k Centroids via K-Means++]
    init --> assign{Step 3: Assignment Step}
    assign --> dist[Compute Squared Euclidean Distance to each Centroid]
    dist --> cluster[Assign each point to nearest Centroid]
    cluster --> update[Step 4: Update Step - Recompute Centroid as Mean of Cluster]
    update --> conv{Step 5: Check Convergence}
    conv -->|Not Converged| assign
    conv -->|Converged| profile[Step 6: Cluster Profiling]
    profile --> size[Compute Cluster Sizes and Proportions]
    profile --> cent[Compute Centroid Vectors and Deviations from Grand Mean]
    profile --> disp[Compute Within-Cluster Standard Deviations and Covariance]
    profile --> met[Compute WCSS, BCSS, Silhouette, CH and DB Indices]
    size --> label[Step 7: Cluster Interpretation and Labeling]
    cent --> label
    disp --> label
    met --> label
    label --> report[Final Cluster Report for Stakeholders]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cluster Profiling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input [Input Layer]
        A1[Converged K-Means Model]
        A2[Original Feature Matrix X]
    end
    subgraph profiling [Profiling Engine]
        B1[Size and Proportion Module]
        B2[Centroid and Median Module]
        B3[Dispersion Module]
        B4[Deviation Profile Module]
        B5[Validation Metrics Module]
    end
    subgraph output [Interpretation Layer]
        C1[Cluster Descriptors Table]
        C2[Signature Feature Ranking]
        C3[Cluster Personas and Labels]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    A1 --> B5
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **Cluster Profiling** and **Cluster Interpretation** in the context of K-Means clustering. Why are these steps necessary after the algorithm converges?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cluster Profiling** is the post-convergence analytical process of quantitatively characterizing each cluster through descriptive statistics — specifically the **centroid vector $\mu_j$**, the **within-cluster standard deviation $\sigma_j$**, the **size $n_j$** and the **proportion $p_j$**.

**Cluster Interpretation** is the qualitative step of assigning a human-readable, domain-meaningful label to a numerically-defined cluster (e.g., "Premium Customers" or "Defective Production Runs") based on the deviation profile $\delta_j = \mu_j - \bar{x}$.

These steps are necessary because K-Means only produces an unlabeled integer partition $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ — without profiling and interpretation, the output has no actionable business or engineering meaning. Profiling transforms raw numbers into insight, and interpretation communicates the insight to non-technical stakeholders. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Explain the **Elbow Method** for choosing the optimal number of clusters $k$ in K-Means. What is its limitation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Elbow Method** plots the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / Inertia)** as a function of $k$. As $k$ increases, WCSS monotonically decreases — the "elbow" point, where the rate of decrease sharply changes (forming a bend like an arm), is chosen as the optimal $k$.

**Limitation**: The elbow point is often **visually ambiguous** (no clear bend), the method is **subjective**, and it does not account for cluster separation — it only rewards compactness. Alternatives like the **Silhouette Score** or **Gap Statistic** are more rigorous. **(3 marks)**

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
For the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{P_1(2,10), P_2(2,5), P_3(8,4), P_4(5,8), P_5(7,5), P_6(6,4), P_7(1,2), P_8(4,9)\}$, apply the K-Means algorithm with $k = 2$ using the initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2,10)$ and $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5,8)$. Run two iterations and show all distance computations.

Compute squared Euclidean distances from each point to both centroids.

Distance to $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2,10)$: $\Vert (x,y) - (2,10)\Vert^2 = (x-2)^2 + (y-10)^2$
Distance to $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5,8)$: $\Vert (x,y) - (5,8)\Vert^2 = (x-5)^2 + (y-8)^2$

| Point | Dist² to $\mu_1$ | Dist² to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1(2,10)$ | $0$ | $9+4=13$ | $C_1$ |
| $P_2(2,5)$ | $0+25=25$ | $9+9=18$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_3(8,4)$ | $36+36=72$ | $9+16=25$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_4(5,8)$ | $9+4=13$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_5(7,5)$ | $25+25=50$ | $4+9=13$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_6(6,4)$ | $16+36=52$ | $1+16=17$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_7(1,2)$ | $1+64=65$ | $16+36=52$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_8(4,9)$ | $4+1=5$ | $1+1=2$ | $C_2$ |

Result: $C_1 = \{P_1\}$, $C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7, P_8\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step:**
$\mu_1^{(1)} = (2,10)$ (singleton unchanged)
$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{7}\big[(2+8+5+7+6+1+4,\; 5+4+8+5+4+2+9)\big] = \left(\frac{33}{7}, \frac{37}{7}\right) \approx (4.71, 5.29)$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment Step:**

| Point | Dist² to $(2,10)$ | Dist² to $(4.71, 5.29)$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1(2,10)$ | $0$ | $7.34+22.18=29.52$ | $C_1$ |
| $P_2(2,5)$ | $25$ | $7.34+0.084=7.42$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_3(8,4)$ | $72$ | $10.82+1.66=12.48$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_4(5,8)$ | $13$ | $0.084+7.34=7.42$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_5(7,5)$ | $50$ | $5.24+0.084=5.32$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_6(6,4)$ | $52$ | $1.66+1.66=3.32$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_7(1,2)$ | $65$ | $13.76+10.82=24.58$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_8(4,9)$ | $5$ | $0.504+13.76=14.27$ | $C_2$ |

Assignments are **stable** — algorithm converges. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
For the converged solution above, compute:
(i) The final centroids, sizes, and proportions of each cluster.
(ii) The Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS) and the Between-Cluster Sum of Squares (BCSS).
(iii) The grand mean and the deviation profile of each cluster. Assign a meaningful label to each cluster.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Final centroids, sizes, and proportions:**

$\mu_1 = (2, 10)$, $n_1 = 1$, $p_1 = 1/8 = 0.125 = 12.5\%$
$\mu_2 = (33/7, 37/7) \approx (4.71, 5.29)$, $n_2 = 7$, $p_2 = 7/8 = 0.875 = 87.5\%$

**(ii) WCSS and BCSS:**

For $C_1$ (singleton, zero within-cluster distance):
$\text{WCSS}_1 = \Vert (2,10) - (2,10)\Vert^2 = 0$

For $C_2$: with 7 points and centroid $(4.71, 5.29)$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WCSS}_2 &= \sum_{P \in C_2}\Vert P - \mu_2\Vert^2 \\
&= 7.42 + 12.48 + 7.42 + 5.32 + 3.32 + 24.58 + 14.27 \\
&= 74.81
\end{aligned}
$$
$$\text{Total WCSS} = 0 + 74.81 = 74.81$$

Grand mean:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{8}\Big[\sum x_i, \sum y_i\Big] = \left(\frac{2+2+8+5+7+6+1+4}{8}, \frac{10+5+4+8+5+4+2+9}{8}\right) = (4.375, 5.875)$$

BCSS:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCSS} &= 1 \cdot \Vert (2,10) - (4.375, 5.875)\Vert^2 + 7 \cdot \Vert (4.71, 5.29) - (4.375, 5.875)\Vert^2 \\
&= 1 \cdot (5.64 + 17.02) + 7 \cdot (0.11 + 0.34) \\
&= 22.66 + 3.15 = 25.81
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Deviation profile and labels:**

$$
\delta_1 = (2 - 4.375,\; 10 - 5.875) = (-2.375,\; +4.125) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{``High-Y, Low-X Outlier"}$$
$$\delta_2 = (4.71 - 4.375,\; 5.29 - 5.875) = (+0.335,\; -0.585) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{``Central Tendency Group"}$$

**Labels:** $C_1$ = "**High-Dominant Outlier**" (only 12.5% of data, distinctive on both axes); $C_2$ = "**Mainstream Cluster**" (87.5% of data, near the grand mean). **(7 marks)**

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
Define the **Silhouette Coefficient** $s(i)$ for a data point $i$. Given a 2-D point $P(3,4)$ belonging to cluster $C_1 = \{(1,1), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5)\}$ with nearest other cluster $C_2 = \{(10,10), (11,11), (12,12)\}$, compute $s(P)$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The silhouette coefficient of point $i$ is:
$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$$

where:
- $a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_{current}\vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_{current}, j \neq i} d(i,j)$ = mean distance of $i$ to all *other* points in its own cluster.
- $b(i) = \min_{C \neq C_{current}} \frac{1}{\vert C \vert}\sum_{j \in C} d(i,j)$ = mean distance of $i$ to the points in the *nearest other* cluster.

**Step 1 — Compute $a(P)$:**
Distances from $P(3,4)$ to other points in $C_1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P, (1,1)) &= \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 \\
d(P, (2,3)) &= \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(P, (4,5)) &= \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
\end{aligned}
$$
$$a(P) = \frac{3.606 + 1.414 + 1.414}{3} = \frac{6.434}{3} \approx 2.145$$

**Step 2 — Compute $b(P)$:**
Distances from $P(3,4)$ to points in $C_2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P, (10,10)) &= \sqrt{49+36} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.220 \\
d(P, (11,11)) &= \sqrt{64+49} = \sqrt{113} \approx 10.630 \\
d(P, (12,12)) &= \sqrt{81+64} = \sqrt{145} \approx 12.042
\end{aligned}
$$
$$b(P) = \frac{9.220 + 10.630 + 12.042}{3} = \frac{31.892}{3} \approx 10.631$$

**Step 3 — Compute $s(P)$:**
$$s(P) = \frac{10.631 - 2.145}{\max\{2.145,\; 10.631\}} = \frac{8.486}{10.631} \approx 0.798$$

**Interpretation:** $s(P) \approx 0.80$ — very close to 1, indicating that $P$ is **very well-matched** to its current cluster $C_1$ and very far from the next cluster $C_2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
Explain the **Calinski–Harabasz Index** and the **Davies–Bouldin Index** as tools for validating K-Means clusters. State which one is maximized and which one is minimized, and justify why.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Calinski–Harabasz Index (CH) — also called the Variance Ratio Criterion:**
$$\text{CH}(k) = \frac{\text{BCSS}/(k-1)}{\text{WCSS}/(n-k)}$$

This is essentially an F-statistic-like ratio: **between-cluster dispersion** (numerator) divided by **within-cluster dispersion** (denominator).

- **Maximized** when clusters are well-separated (large BCSS) and internally compact (small WCSS).
- **Higher CH = better clustering** for a given $k$.
- It is fast to compute and works well for convex, globular clusters.

**Davies–Bouldin Index (DB):**
$$\text{DB} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} \left( \frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}} \right)$$

where $S_i$ is the average distance of cluster $i$'s points from its centroid (a measure of cluster scatter), and $M_{ij}$ is the distance between centroids $i$ and $j$.

- **Minimized** when each cluster is compact ($S_i$ small) AND far from other clusters ($M_{ij}$ large).
- **Lower DB = better clustering**.

**Why the two indices complement each other:**
- CH uses the **global** variance decomposition (one number for the whole partition).
- DB uses **pairwise** cluster similarity (penalizes clusters that are too close to each other).
- Together, they help in (i) choosing optimal $k$, (ii) comparing two K-Means solutions, and (iii) detecting overlapping or poorly-separated clusters.

**Justification summary:** CH is **maximized** because the F-statistic in classical ANOVA tests *between-group* signal against *within-group* noise — high between and low within means strong clustering. DB is **minimized** because the formula $\frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}}$ measures the *worst-case* similarity of cluster $i$ to any other cluster; minimizing it forces clusters to be both tight and well-separated. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Zones for this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize features** (especially when features have different units like "income in ₹" vs. "age in years") — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always apply Z-score normalization first.
> 2. **Conflating WCSS with TSS** — students often compute WCSS incorrectly by summing all $\Vert x - \bar{x}\Vert^2$ instead of $\Vert x - \mu_j\Vert^2$. Remember: WCSS uses **cluster centroids**, not the grand mean.
> 3. **Stopping after one iteration** — full-mark solutions must show **at least two iterations** OR explicitly demonstrate **convergence** by showing that cluster membership is unchanged.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step** — after numerical computation, the **deviation profile $\delta_j$** and the **cluster label** are mandatory. Pure computation without interpretation is considered incomplete (loses 2–3 marks).
> 5. **Misnaming metrics** — confusing "Silhouette Score" (where higher is better) with "Davies–Bouldin Index" (where lower is better) is a frequent error.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — K-Means assumes **spherical, equal-variance clusters**. State this assumption explicitly in derivations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — K-Means Profiling and Interpretation**

- **Algorithm type**: Unsupervised, partitioning, centroid-based, iterative refinement.
- **Objective function**: Minimize WCSS $= \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert^2$.
- **Convergence**: Guaranteed to a **local minimum** (not global). Use **multiple restarts** or **K-Means++** to escape poor local minima.
- **Initialization**: **K-Means++** is the gold standard — it spreads initial centroids apart using $P(\text{select } x) \propto D(x)^2$.
- **Feature scaling**: **Z-score standardization** is **mandatory** when features have different units or variances.
- **Profiling outputs (per cluster)**: size $n_j$, proportion $p_j$, centroid $\mu_j$, median $m_j$, within-cluster std $\sigma_j$, within-cluster covariance $W_j$, deviation profile $\delta_j = \mu_j - \bar{x}$.
- **Validation indices**: CH (maximize), DB (minimize), Silhouette (maximize), Gap Statistic (maximize), Inertia/WCSS (minimize, but with elbow rule).
- **TSS Decomposition (Law of Total Variance)**: $\text{TSS} = \text{WCSS} + \text{BCSS}$ — this is a **guaranteed identity**, not an approximation. KTU exams frequently test this.
- **Interpretive workflow**: Profile → Identify signature features (largest $\vert \delta_{jl} \vert / \sigma_l$) → Assign persona label → Report to stakeholders.
- **Practical K-Means Weaknesses**: Sensitive to outliers (use K-Medoids instead), assumes spherical clusters (use DBSCAN/GMM for arbitrary shapes), requires pre-specifying $k$ (use elbow/silhouette).
- **Common KTU 14-mark answer structure**: (1) State assumption and metric, (2) Initialize centroids, (3) Show iteration-by-iteration distance tables, (4) Compute centroids, (5) Check convergence, (6) Compute profiling statistics, (7) Interpret and label.
- **Memory aid**: "**A**ssign, **U**pdate, **C**onverge, **P**rofile, **I**nterpret, **L**abel" = **AUC-PIL** mnemonic for the K-Means pipeline.
